title: Cypress Creek secures US$3.5 billion to fund 1.63GW/1.9GWh solar-plus-storage project
snippet: “US independent power producer (IPP) Cypress Creek Energy has secured US$3.5 billion in financing to support the development of a 1.63GW/1.9GWh solar-plus-storage project in Arkansas. The financing will support the first two phases of the three-phase Steel River Energy Center project, which comprises 2.45GW of solar PV and 2.9GWh of battery storage, making it one of the largest solar-plus-storage projects in the US. The entire project is expected to be completed by 2029.”

title: MN8 commissions 260 MW of new solar capacity to supply clean electricity for Microsoft operations in Texas and North Carolina
snippet: “MN8 Energy revealed to the public that the newly operational projects include the 120 MW Long Point Solar facility in Texas and the 140 MW Herbert Solar facility in North Carolina. Together, the installations deliver 260 MW of solar generation capacity across two major U.S. electricity markets. The projects have been developed under long-term power purchase agreements (PPAs) with Microsoft. Data centers are known to use massive amounts of electricity, so MN8 will assist the technology entity to secure additional renewable electricity for its ever-growing operations. ”

title: Group forms to oppose proposed solar project in Keno
snippet: “The Keno Wildlands Alliance, composed of local residents, has established a 501(c)(4) political advocacy group to oppose Ørsted’s proposed Klamath Falls Energy Center. “We’re not anti-solar; we just don’t think it should be on this land,” said Joyce Furlong, who represents the alliance… The Klamath Falls Energy Center is a proposed 400-megawatt solar farm with a 400-megawatt battery storage system. The project is currently under review by the Oregon Department of Energy’s State Solar Siting Commission.”

title: After nearly two decades, this massive New Mexico wind project is now powering California
snippet: “The largest wind energy project in U.S. history is now online, delivering power from a massive array in New Mexico to Arizona and California — and signaling a new era for sending clean electricity across the West. Nearly two decades in the making, the estimated $11-billion SunZia project from Pattern Energy is now fully operational, company officials said Thursday. It’s made up of 916 turbines that can produce up to 3.65 gigawatts of electricity, making it potentially more powerful than the Hoover Dam. ”

title: Solar generations surpasses natural gas in California after panel installs surge
snippet: “Utility-scale solar generation in California exceeded power from gas during 82% of the days this year through May, according to a Tuesday report from the US Energy Information Association. While solar has supplied more power to the state’s grid for short periods in the past, this marks the first year when average generation in the first five months has outpaced the fossil fuel that’s the biggest source of US electricity.”

title: Ellery nears moratorium extensions for wind, solar, BESS
snippet: The Ellery Town Board is poised to extend its moratorium on wind, solar, and battery energy storage system projects for up to one year. Public hearings were held June 9. The moratoriums will last six months after passage and can be extended twice for three months each. The town is drafting a local law for wind energy and considering one for BESS. For solar, the board will require that components be recycled as part of decommissioning. Solar projects in sensitive areas near Chautauqua Lake, schools, Long Point State Park, the village of Bemus Point, and wildlife management areas will face higher standards and a 2,500-foot setback from non-participating property lines. Once a special use permit is granted, developers will have six months to obtain a building permit and one year to complete the project. Town attorney Neil Robinson reminded the board that the local law “can’t be so restrictive that it prevents development of solar farms.”

title: NextEra to pay $150M to settle charges related to Florida political misconduct allegations
snippet: NextEra Energy agreed to pay $150 million to settle a shareholder lawsuit in federal court in Florida. The case involved allegations of political misconduct, including funding “ghost” candidates to defeat state lawmakers and spying on a reporter, in the context of NextEra’s proposed $67 billion merger with Dominion Energy. The settlement is covered by insurance and is the largest securities class action settlement in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Florida since 1995.

title: Community continues to voice concerns on expansive solar projects in Dubois County
snippet: Dubois County residents are raising ongoing concerns about large solar projects, including fire safety, road access, lack of independent county oversight, and inadequate financial protections. Commissioners have limited authority to stop projects due to state law but are developing new zoning ordinances and considering stricter setbacks. A moratorium remains in place on the second phase of the EDP Renewables Duff Solar Project while the county finalizes land-use rules.

title: Unfounded health concerns in Michigan are powering a solar backlash
snippet: St. Clair County adopted a novel public health regulation last year that set limits on solar development and battery storage, encouraged by the county’s medical director who warned of noise, visual pollution, and potential contamination. County Commissioner (now Chair) Steven Simasko accepted the medical opinion “as a good standard for the protection of the public health of our citizens.” The policy included a $25,000 nonrefundable fee and violations punishable by up to six months in prison. In February, a circuit court judge ruled the health regulation “invalid, null, and void,” but county officials unanimously voted to appeal.

title: Plymouth County hits pause on data centers with 1-year moratorium
snippet: Plymouth County Supervisors approved a one-year “revocable moratorium” on the construction of data centers and industrial battery storage facilities. The vote was 4-0. The moratorium aims to give the county time to prepare rules on construction, water and power usage, and noise issues. Supervisor Chairman Don Kass said, “It’s become more of a local issue. I myself had citizens come up to me and express their concern regarding these kind of facilities. So, we’ve decided we really need to be proactive and get ahead of this issue.”

title: Marathon County to hold listening session on proposed wind farm
snippet: Marathon County hosted a listening session on June 17 at the Lake View Conference Center at 6 p.m. to gather resident feedback on the proposed Hub City Wind Farm by Alliant Energy (28–38 turbines, ~150 MW). The project could power 45,000 homes and bring over $22 million in revenue. The county’s advisory committee is collecting input to inform negotiations with the developer. Committee chair John Robinson said, “Our (job) is not to pass judgment on the merits of the project, but rather collect the information” and “We’re trying to be inclusive and get as much input as we can.”

title: Big battery farms encounter resistance across western Washington
snippet: Community opposition to utility-scale battery energy storage systems has grown across western Washington, leading multiple counties and cities (including King, Skagit, Pierce, Kitsap, and Thurston counties, plus Snoqualmie, Carnation, North Bend, Puyallup, and Duvall) to enact or extend six-month to one-year moratoriums on new BESS permits. Concerns center on lithium-ion battery fire risks, toxic smoke, noise from cooling fans, and impacts on neighborhoods, schools, and farmland. Anacortes resident Elke Siller Macartney said, “There’s a dawning awareness of the awful effects of BESS installations such as thermal runaway fires and toxic smoke, and the horrible noise from cooling fans… It’s the height of arrogance for these companies to pretend to install these things for the supposed good of all.”

title: State goals, local impacts
snippet: Inside the fight over where large-scale solar should go in NY: New York’s push for large-scale solar is creating conflicts in rural upstate counties as projects like Flat Creek Solar (300 MW on 3,000 acres in Montgomery County) and Mill Point Solar move forward under the Office of Renewable Energy Siting (ORES). Residents and local officials in Montgomery and Washington counties are raising concerns about cumulative impacts on farmland, wildlife, drainage, property values, and the loss of local control.

title: States claim victory as Trump admin ends wind court fight
snippet: The Trump administration dropped its appeal of a court ruling that struck down its “wind directive” pausing new onshore and offshore wind projects. The 1st U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals granted the Interior Department’s request to dismiss the appeal. New York Attorney General Letitia James said the ruling protects projects that “will create jobs, strengthen our economy, and bring down New Yorkers’ electric bills.” Massachusetts Attorney General Andrea Joy Campbell said the court “correctly protected those critical investments from the Trump administration’s unlawful order.”

title: Zeeland Twp neighbors fight to stay in solar farm approval process
snippet: Neighbors in Zeeland and Jamestown townships are fighting to remain as intervenors in the Michigan Public Service Commission case for RWE Americas’ proposed 200-megawatt Silver Maple Solar Farm on 1,900 acres. RWE’s attorneys moved to dismiss some non-adjacent neighbors. Administrative Law Judge James Varchetti will rule on intervenor status June 25. Neighbor Valerie Driesenga said, “We’re an adjacent landowner, so we immediately have the right to be part of the case.” Neighbors are holding a pancake breakfast fundraiser on June 20 to raise money for legal representation.

title: Solar-friendly bill would again cut Kern’s property tax receipts
snippet: Senate Bill 1329 would change how large-scale solar projects are assessed for property taxes by valuing only tangible assets and excluding power purchase agreements, tax credits, and subsidies. The change would reduce tax revenue for counties like Kern. Kern County Board Chairman Phillip Peters said, “The state continues to pass costs, unfunded mandates and other financial obligations down to the county.” State Sen. Shannon Grove (R-Bakersfield) strongly opposed the bill, saying she is “tired of providing the energy and using our energy and our resources, and now you won’t even let us have the tax revenue.”

title: ‘Different this time’
snippet: Wind farms and transmission lines poised to come to northern Maine: Maine’s Public Utilities Commission is moving forward with a new RFP (issued December 2025, bids closing June 12, 2026) to select wind and solar projects plus transmission infrastructure in Aroostook County to deliver at least 1,200 MW to the New England grid. The effort is a regional collaboration with Connecticut, Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and Vermont to share costs and benefits. Philip Bartlett, PUC chairman, stated, “This RFP builds on prior stakeholder input and regional transmission planning to move renewable energy projects in northern Maine from concept to reality.” Jack Shapiro of the Natural Resources Council of Maine noted, “This time they started working with all the other states in advance… It’s basically the biggest opportunity that we have for low-cost renewable energy in the medium term.”

title: Landlord Sues Vineyard Offshore for Missed Rent Payments
snippet: BP Hancock filed suit in Suffolk County Superior Court claiming Vineyard Offshore (an affiliate of Vineyard Wind backer Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners) owes more than $1.2 million in unpaid rent, fees, and security deposit replenishment for 28,370 square feet of office space on the 18th floor at 200 Clarendon Street in Boston. The tenant has been in default since March 1, 2026. Attorney Kate Moran Carter stated, “Tenant has been in default since March 1, 2026, after it failed to pay Rent and other charges due under the Lease and to replenish its security deposit.”

title: Town of Franklin proposes one-year moratorium on battery storage projects
snippet: The Town of Franklin (Franklin County) is considering a one-year moratorium on battery energy storage systems due to concerns over lithium-ion battery fires and long-term pollution from decommissioned batteries. A public meeting is scheduled for Wednesday at 5 p.m. at town hall. This follows a trend of more than 30 New York municipalities enacting BESS bans or moratoriums.

title: Rockingham supervisors reject solar farm proposal
snippet: The Rockingham County Board of Supervisors voted 4-0 to deny a special use permit for a solar farm near Timberville on agricultural land roughly a quarter-mile from Plains Elementary School. Concerns included stormwater runoff, rocky terrain making construction difficult, conflict with the county’s agriculture-support goals, high municipal water use for cooling, and potential negative impacts on property values. Resident David Mewellski said, “They’re not going to be able to just pound poles in the ground… The property they’re planning on putting it on is on nothing but rock.”

title: Sedgwick County weighing second temporary ban on solar energy projects in 3 years
snippet: The Sedgwick County Commission is considering a six-month moratorium on new solar projects (with possible extension). Commissioners cited the need for more time after reviewing two recent applications, concerns over protest petitions, proximity to Clearwater’s urban growth area, and infrastructure issues (including Ark 95 and potential south connector roads). Commissioner Jeff Blubaugh stated, “We don’t want to rush this… I want to make sure that we’ve turned over every stone.” A decision is expected at the June 17 meeting.

title: Apache County P&Z recommends renewable energy moratorium
snippet: The Apache County Planning and Zoning Commission voted 6-2 on June 3 to recommend that the Board of Supervisors adopt a temporary moratorium on new renewable energy conditional use permit applications while the county rewrites its renewable energy ordinance. Public concerns raised include impacts on public safety, emergency communications, water sources, roads, cultural/scenic resources, noise, and proximity to homes. A separate Black Ridge wind project application was submitted around May 26, adding urgency. The recommendation now goes to the Board of Supervisors for further process including public hearing.

title: [Opinion] Solar lawyer
snippet: Are bans on solar really banned?: “HB711, which amended VA Code 15.2-2288.8 (“Special exceptions for solar voltaic projects”), has been called the “solar standards” bill. It has also been called a pyrrhic victory because, for all that it does, it does not limit counties’ ability to turn down solar projects…The core point is not complicated. HB 711 gives covered solar projects a required path to local review. Virginia counties still control the ultimate vote. They can still consider local impacts. They can still impose lawful conditions. They can still deny projects that do not satisfy the applicable standards.”

title: Cypress Creek scores USD 3.5bn in funds for Arkansas solar-BESS giant
snippet: “US renewable energy developer and operator Cypress Creek Renewables has secured USD 3.5 billion (EUR 3.03bn) in financing for part of its gigawatt-scale Steel River Energy Center, a huge solar-and-storage project in Arkansas…When all phases are completed, the Steel River Energy Center will comprise 2.45 GW of photovoltaic (PV) farms and 2.9 GWh of battery energy storage systems (BESS)...The solar-storage complex will be located in Mississippi County in the northeastern part of Arkansas. Phases 1 and 2 will be equipped with domestically-made panels by First Solar and will be operated under a virtual power purchase agreement (PPA) with an investment-grade corporate counterparty.”

title: Grundy County intends to appeal appellate court’s ruling on solar panels
snippet: “The Grundy County Board approved a new solar guideline Tuesday that requires solar developers to have concrete plans in place before receiving permits from the county…The Third District Appellate Court ruled against Grundy County on Tuesday, March 10, in the case of Equity Solar Illinois vs. County of Grundy. According to the court document, the dispute between Equity Solar and Grundy County arose when Grundy County rejected special use permit requests, and the plaintiffs filed a mandamus action, which was granted by the Grundy County Circuit Court…The county will require more intensive financial and legal protections through explicit fee formulas, higher insurance limits and irrevocable financial assurances for both decommissioning and reclamation…The circuit court ordered Grundy County to issue the permits, and the county appealed. It lost its appeal, but it plans to appeal further.”

title: Massive clean energy project caught in legal crosswinds at WA Supreme Court
snippet: “Opponents of the Horse Heaven wind project contend that, in looking to act expeditiously, the state Energy Facility Site Evaluation Council and former Democratic Gov. Jay Inslee failed to properly account for the project’s negative impacts on the environment, surrounding communities and tribal cultural resources…Tri-Cities CARES, Benton County and the Yakama Nation filed separate petitions seeking to overturn the final decision issued in October 2024. Justices consolidated the petitions and will hear arguments at 10 a.m. Thursday. At stake is the future of a massive wind, solar and battery storage project envisioned on roughly 72,500 acres along Interstate 82 south of the Tri-Cities. More broadly, if the court sides with the project’s foes, it will seriously crimp state efforts to reduce Washington’s reliance on power generated from fossil fuels…Opponents contend the state’s Administrative Procedures Act was not followed and that the environment and wildlife are inadequately protected. Residents say nothing was done to address interference with their views of the Horse Heaven hills…The Yakama Nation contends the final decision resulted in a project that would have significantly greater harm to wildlife and their cultural resources compared to the council’s initial recommendation. They also said there was inadequate consultation between the tribe and the council.”

title: NY power grid squeezed by aging plants, extreme weather, and data centers
snippet: NYISO report: “The New York Independent System Operator warned on Tuesday that extreme weather and new corporate users with high demands are shrinking electricity reserves, leaving the state’s grid close to a shortage on the hottest and coldest days of the year…Despite the warnings about electric grid reliability, New York achieved a renewable energy milestone on June 3. According to NYISO, the record for solar power generation was broken during the noon hour that day, with solar panels generating 5,661 megawatts—about 29% of the entire statewide demand. Everyday residential New Yorkers generated most of that through small, private rooftop systems called “behind-the-meter” solar. Those systems created 5,131 megawatts, while large commercial solar farms generated just 530 megawatts.”

title: Waller County Judge Posts, Comal Commissioner Shares, Explanation Why Counties Can’t Stop Developers, Data Centers
snippet: “Waller County Judge Trey Duhon explained on Facebook yesterday the reasons why counties can’t regulate the explosive growth reshaping Texas communities like Canyon Lake and New Braunfels… ‘I recently received a DM on Facebook from a resident inquiring about starting a petition against a new development because they are concerned that it’s going to affect electricity, roads, and flooding. This raises a very good topic for discussion, especially in light of current hot-button topics such as data centers, battery-energy storage facilities, solar farms, etc…Counties can only regulate what the Texas legislature has given us the ability to regulate, which honestly is not much. ‘For example, we do not have ‘zoning authority.’ We can not arbitrarily dictate where certain types of developments can be built. We do not have noise-ordinance authority…We can not impose arbitrary rules on any developer without running the risk of exposing the county to liability….As I frequently tell people, if you want our commissioners court to have more authority in terms of regulating these types of things, then your time and energy is probably better spent on communicating with state representatives, state senators, and state officials to give counties more authority to regulate these types of things (like zoning authority, for example).’”

title: Neighbors rally against battery energy facility in Oshtemo Township
snippet: “A rally against a potential battery energy storage system, or BESS, facility that could be built on rural land near homes and agriculture drew a group of more than 100 neighbors to Little Pistol Farms in Oshtemo Township Monday night…Residents formed a group called ‘Say No to BESS,’ urging their local leaders to oppose BESS development, because of a variety of environmental and health concerns related to the technology…The land up against Little Pistol Farms and seven other residential property lines, on Van Kal Avenue, has been eyed by an out of state company, NewEdge Renewable Power. NewEdge Renewable Power has not submitted a formal application, but has previously stated plans to submit an application for the site during the second half of 2026, which is around the same time as the township's moratorium is expected to expire…Oshtemo Township’s one-year moratorium on commercial battery energy storage development applications was approved in November of last year. According to the township attorney, it could be extended. The next Oshtemo Township Planning Commission meeting is scheduled for Thursday, June 25th at 6 pm.”

The Fayette Township Planning Commission voted last week to deny a special land use permit sought by Ranger Power for its proposed Heartwood Solar II and III projects…Wortz added that federal incentives are likely contributing to the continued push for solar projects across Michigan. ‘The federal Solar Tax credit is expiring on December 31, 2027,’ she said. ‘For commercial solar projects to receive this 30% tax credit the project must be fully installed and functioning by this date. I believe that is why there is such a push to get these projects through.’ If Ranger Power appeals, Wortz said she hopes state regulators defer to the township's decision.”

title: Allium Solar
snippet: County could lose local control over battery energy storage project: “In August 2025, Boston-based Longroad Energy filed an application to build a 945-acre solar and battery facility north of the Hollister airport…The project, called Allium Solar, would sit north of Hollister on agricultural land bordering Highway 156, across from the Amazon warehouse, and stretching up to Hwy 25. Both the property owner and Longroad describe the site as low-productivity farmland. It would generate 110 megawatts of solar power and store an equivalent capacity in batteries…The county’s current moratorium remains in effect until the ordinance is presented to the board, which was supposed to occur on June 9. The draft ordinance, County Counsel Gregory Priamos said, was expected to be posted June 4 or June 5. However, on June 5, the county released a June 9 board meeting agenda without a draft energy storage ordinance that includes a scheduled public hearing with a staff recommendation to adopt an urgency ordinance to extend the moratorium for more than 10 additional months while staff continues work on an energy storage ordinance.”

title: Rural Showdown
snippet: Fayette Township Slams Brakes On Massive Heartwood Solar Farm: “On Monday night, Fayette Township’s planning commission slammed the brakes on Ranger Power’s Heartwood II solar proposal, voting to deny a special land‑use permit for a 140‑megawatt array that would have stretched across roughly 1,350 acres. The decision capped months of heated public meetings where neighbors, local officials and Hillsdale College warned the project would threaten water resources, wetlands and the township’s agricultural character…Zoning consultants from Carlisle/Wortman formally recommended denial, citing deficient wetland protection and the inappropriate conversion of productive farmland. The commission’s motion to reject the permit echoed those findings and, as reported by WTVB, also pointed to gaps in the applicant’s emergency‑response planning, the planned removal of dozens of acres of trees and other impacts to the township’s rural landscape.”

title: Trump administration blocks wind farm development in Illinois and across the nation
snippet: “Billions of dollars in wind farm projects all over Illinois are on hold, including two projects in McLean County, two in Peoria County, and one in Tazewell County…Until last August, getting these [FAA (Federal Aviation Administration) compliance] certificates was routine. The FAA automatically kicks applications to the Department of Defense to see if there is a risk to military readiness, said a source knowledgeable about the process…Mitigation cases stopped getting approved starting last August, said a background source. In late April, even the easy cases stopped receiving certificates. Now, no wind farm project in the nation that didn’t already have permit approval is moving forward, said multiple sources…The American Clean Power Association estimated not allowing the U.S. to construct new wind projects would cost the national electricity grid an extra $361 billion over the next 25 years because of higher prices based on demand.”

title: Wind energy critics rally at Capitol, warn of a ‘Wyoming Wind Wall’
snippet: “Rather than take aim at any one particular wind energy project, rally organizers called on decisionmakers to consider the bigger picture: the cumulative impact of converting mostly undeveloped, agricultural landscapes into semi-industrialized zones with wind towers that are regarded by many as a blight on Wyoming’s open spaces…Cheyenne area resident Wendy Volk, who organized the event, has implored state officials to consider individual wind energy projects as part of a larger industrial infrastructure that’s morphing into a corridor of towers, power lines and electrical substations from Medicine Bow to Cheyenne to Douglas.”

title: US court dismisses GE Vineyard appeal
snippet: A Massachusetts judge has rejected GE Vernova’s request to throw out a previous order requiring the turbine supplier to continue working on CIP-Iberdrola’s 806MW Vineyard Wind 1 array off the coast of Massachusetts…Justice Peter Krupp of the Massachusetts Superior Court ruled in a court order late last week that the activation of Vineyard Wind’s power purchase agreement with the state of Massachusetts and declaration of COD does not mean that GE Vernova can now walk away from the project…[Krupp] added: “The fact that VW declared a particular milestone under the PPAs does not mean that the (turbines) are functioning at capacity, as designed, or without substantial need for repair and improvement; or that (GE Vernova) does not have specialised expertise needed to bring about timely repair and improvement to VW’s operational capacity.”

title: Supreme Court of Ohio reverses permit for Oak Run Solar site
snippet: “The Supreme Court of Ohio has cut down a previously approved permit for Madison County’s Oak Run Solar project, which would have been the largest solar installation in the history of the state. The court’s majority claim that the project’s permit application did not meet the Ohio Power Siting Board’s (OPSB) submission requirements in full…In the court’s decision, the justices order the siting board to “more thoroughly address” some of the purely visual impacts of the project. However, it is crucial to note that this decision does not outright kill the Oak Run Solar project, and that the opportunity is still open to solar developers around Ohio and the surrounding area.”

title: Skagit County attempts to plan for clean energy future
snippet: “As proposed energy facilities cause conflict throughout the state, Skagit County is working on more specific development regulations for the projects that will inevitably come. Earlier this year, Skagit County received a $770,000 grant from the state Department of Commerce, funded through the Climate Commitment Act, to develop local clean energy development regulations and prepare an environmental impact statement, which requires significant public outreach. Similar grants were awarded to Spokane, King and Kittitas counties and the City of Richland…In Skagit County especially, where restrictive zoning to protect local agriculture means many parcels are relatively affordable, energy developers have been making overtures in the area for several years. The county has already seen two large battery facility proposals: one west of Mount Vernon that went through the county permitting process, and another outside of Sedro-Woolley that is still working its way through the state siting process, which supersedes county control...The project is expected to wrap up by May 2027, with opportunities for public comment throughout the process.”

title: Defying Trump, California continues to bet big on offshore wind

title: [Hillclimber Solar Withdraws Application]

title: [Opinion] Defense Department rides with Trump’s mendacious assault on wind energy “The Defense Department, undoubtedly at Trump’s insistence, is working to achieve through stonewalling what the administration could not through other shenanigans
snippet: to stop or significantly slow the development of wind energy in the U.S. In Texas, 54 such projects are among 165 nationwide in limbo as the Defense Department claims to still be reviewing them to ensure they don’t impair national security. But those reviews, triggered by proposed structures 200 feet and taller and which federal law requires to be completed within 60 days, have ground to a halt with no reasonable explanation. Not only has the department not cleared a wind project since August, in April it canceled all meetings with wind developers waiting for action, the American Clean Power Association reported. Jason Grumet, CEO of the ACP, told the Associated Press that the backlog is unprecedented, calling it a “de facto moratorium” on new land-based wind energy.”

title: Ticonderoga residents raise concerns over potential lithium battery plant
snippet: Residents in Ticonderoga voiced strong opposition to a proposed lithium battery energy storage facility, citing environmental impacts, water usage for cooling, potential runoff into local water systems, and long-term cleanup responsibilities. Town leaders are considering a six-month moratorium to review local laws and scientific data on battery storage. Town Supervisor James O’Bryan stated, “Moratorium obviously gets a chance to look at not only our local laws, but also any other facts, any other sciences that revolve around battery energy storage.” Resident Carol Ramundo said, “My stance is definitely no,” and added concerns about water use: “These plants also use a tremendous amount of water to cool their batteries. So where is that going to go?” A public hearing will be held to gather input before a vote.

title: Chateaugay officials talk Wind Repowering Project
snippet: Town officials in Chateaugay (Franklin and Clinton counties) held a public hearing on the proposed repowering of the existing Chateaugay Wind Park from 106.5 MW to 116.5 MW by Valcour Chateaugay Newco LLC (AES subsidiary). The project involves replacing older turbines with larger, more efficient models on ~803 acres and is under review by the Office of Renewable Energy Siting (ORES). The project is expected to power 21,009 homes and continue economic contributions (over $14 million since 2009). Town Supervisor Donald Bilow noted the ORES process has been challenging. Fire Chief Gerry Blow praised AES as “great neighbors.” Landowner Bruce Bennett expressed strong support, saying AES helped fund his children’s college education. Written comments were due May 15, 2026.

Hillclimber Solar… is seeking approval to develop a 156.5-megawatt solar and battery storage facility in Urbana Township in Champaign County… Despite the application advancing to the hearing stage, Ohio Power Siting Board staff has recommended the project be denied… The staff recommendation is considered a significant development in the case, as OPSB staff reviews applications for compliance with state standards before making recommendations to the board.” [Peak of Ohio, 05/13/2026, link to article ]

title: COMMISSIONERS START PROCESS FOR SALDO AMENDMENT FOR SOLAR, WIND FARMS
snippet: “Earlier today, the Indiana County Commissioners approved starting the process to add a solar and wind development amendment to the county’s Subdivision and Land Development Ordinance. Josh Krug with the office of planning and development said that the amendments will deal with large-scale commercial solar and wind farms. The amendment will soon be made available online, at libraries throughout the county, municipal offices and at the Planning and Development and Commissioners offices, and there will be a 45-day period for the public to examine it. There will also be a public hearing on the matter, but that has not been scheduled yet.”

title: Rockdale County extends ban on data centers
snippet: “The Rockdale County Board of Commissioners on Tuesday night extended a moratorium on data centers and battery energy storage systems. The move follows an initial 180-day moratorium enacted in March 2026 to evaluate the cumulative impacts of high-water-use facilities. title: Huntingburg Council approves Crossvine Solar Project settlement
snippet: “The Huntingburg City Council voted unanimously at the regular meeting held on Tuesday to approve a settlement agreement with Crossvine AES, despite strong opposition from residents concerned about safety risks and property values. The decision came after the city’s special counsel, Joshua Claybourn, warned that Huntingburg’s moratorium on solar development sits ‘on thin ice’ legally and that litigation against the multi-billion dollar corporation would likely fail while costing the city hundreds of thousands of dollars. The settlement includes several concessions from AES, including a six-foot berm or cmomparable vegetation screening along neighboring properties, increased setbacks of 100 feet from property lines and 250 feet from residences, and incident monitoring equipment for the battery energy storage systems.”

title: Snoqualmie City Council Appoints New Member, Advances BESS Moratorium, Names Police Chief Finalist
snippet: “At the meeting, Mayor Mayhew introduced a moratorium ordinance on battery energy storage facilities within Snoqualmie city limits. He noted that a Snoqualmie moratorium would have no effect on the proposed Jupiter Power project, which is located in unincorporated King County, but said it was appropriate for the city to move forward with its own legislative process regarding whether such facilities should be permitted within city limits. He also said the continued focus on whether Snoqualmie should adopt a moratorium had become a distraction from the city’s work in the King County process…The council went into a 20-minute executive session with legal counsel to discuss the ordinance before voting to waive the standard 72-hour notification requirement, allowing the ordinance to be read in for the first time… The council voted 5-1, with Councilmember Washington the lone dissenting vote, preferring to act on the ordinance that night rather than refer it to committee.”

title: Attorney General James and Governor Hochul intervene to protect Sunrise Wind project
snippet: New York Attorney General Letitia James and Governor Kathy Hochul moved to intervene in a federal lawsuit seeking to vacate approvals for the 924-megawatt Sunrise Wind offshore wind project, which is expected to power 600,000 New York homes. The project is set to begin delivering power this year and become fully operational next year, providing more than $875 million in economic benefits and supporting over 3,500 jobs. Attorney General James stated, “Contrary to the president’s claims, wind energy is essential for our economy, our environment, and our electric grid… This administration has already tried to shut down Sunrise Wind once, and we will not leave the future of this critical project in its hands.” Governor Hochul added, “Offshore wind projects like Sunrise Wind will not only provide needed clean energy that will help our environment, but also create good-paying union jobs, benefit our local economies, and help secure our energy independence.”

title: Confusing ballot wording may have tipped Ohio vote on renewables ban
snippet: Voters in Richland County, Ohio, upheld a ban on utility-scale solar and wind in 11 townships (53%–47%) on May 5, 2026, but exit polling suggests the result would have flipped to 54% favoring repeal if ballot wording had been clearer. The confusing language made a “yes” vote mean keeping the ban and a “no” vote mean repealing it. Campaign leader Morgan Carroll said, “The question of who gets to decide what happens on private land in Richland County did not end tonight.”

title: Berkeley County panel advances plan to lift Cross moratorium for 1,500-acre solar farm
snippet: The Berkeley County Land Use Committee advanced a plan to lift a large-scale development moratorium covering nearly 7,500 acres in the Cross community to allow rezoning for RWE Clean Energy’s 198-megawatt Sandy Run Solar project on 1,500 acres. The full council will consider the proposal in two weeks. Resident Robert Barrett said, “Cross is not an industrial community. It’s a retirement and recreational community… We don’t want that big industry in there.” Developer Peter Stalter noted the site is at least a mile from the nearest public right-of-way.

title: Workers left in limbo after permit delays stalls wind energy project
snippet: A major wind energy project in Knox County, Illinois, has come to a halt due to federal permitting delays involving the FAA and Department of Defense. Construction crews have begun dismantling a mobile concrete batch plant and lay-down yard. Labor leaders report that about 25 workers from the laborers union and another dozen from other trades were laid off immediately. Dane Simpson, Renewable Energy Director for Great Plains LECET, stated, “They were two to three weeks into the project, and they really felt like the rug got pulled out from under them.” The project was expected to create around 300 construction jobs and generate roughly $48 million in tax revenue for Knox County over its 35-year life cycle. NextEra Energy Resources has not commented on when construction might resume.

title: Quincy Township Adopts Strict Battery Storage Ordinance
snippet: “Quincy Township officials recently [unanimously] adopted a restrictive new ordinance for battery energy storage systems (BESS) to maintain local authority over utility-scale energy projects… The measure…restricts large-scale battery facilities exclusively to industrial zones as a special land use. This strategic move is designed to comply with Michigan’s Public Act 233, a 2023 law that allows the state to override local zoning for renewable energy projects unless a community has a ‘compatible’ local ordinance already in place. The new rules come in response to significant community opposition regarding the proposed 180-megawatt Santina Energy project on Boone Road, which residents feared could lead to fire hazards and groundwater contamination.”

title: Oshtemo hosts discussions after battery storage prompts controversy
snippet: “Oshtemo Township is launching Community Conversations to give residents more time to chat with elected officials… The first Community Conversation last week focused on battery energy storage systems. The township passed a moratorium to block the development of battery storage facilities after NewEdge Power approached the township about building a 260-megawatt battery storage facility in 2024. Multiple community members have spoken out against the proposed battery storage facility. The battery storage facility was among reasons why some residents supported a campaign to recall several board members. New Edge hasn’t made any formal development proposals.”

title: Rules approved for data centers and battery storage – The Commercial Review
snippet: “Jay County Commissioners approved two new ordinances regulating battery storage facilities and data centers and amended existing ordinances regarding commercial solar and wind farms Monday. They also repealed a one-year moratorium on new wind farm developments and retroactively ended one-year moratoriums on new solar farm and battery storage facility developments. Jay/Portland Building and Planning has been working with the county plan commission on drafting zoning regulations for battery storage and data center facilities, as well as updating zoning restrictions for solar and wind farms, for about a year… Moratoriums on new solar farm and battery storage developments in Jay County ended April 28. The moratorium on new wind farm developments was set to remain in effect through July 21… With the ordinance approved Monday, [BESS] facilities in Jay County must have 500-foot setbacks from property lines. Equipment also needs to be enclosed within a 7-foot security fence set back 50 feet from the property line. Permit fees are $2,500 per megawatt.”

title: County enacts one-year moratorium on solar, wind projects
snippet: “Morrison County commissioners approved a one-year moratorium on certain solar and wind energy projects May 5, following a recommendation from the Morrison County Planning Commission. The interim ordinance applies to community solar energy systems, solar farms and wind energy conversion systems under county jurisdiction. It gives the county time to study how larger renewable energy projects fit into its comprehensive plan, zoning ordinance and land-use policies.”

title: Watching Snoqualmie, North Bend Pursues Moratorium on Battery Storage
snippet: “As the proposed Cascadia Ridge battery energy storage facility near Snoqualmie Ridge continues through King County’s permitting process, the City of North Bend is taking steps to ensure it doesn’t face a similar situation on its own doorstep. At an April 28 work study, North Bend city staff and council members discussed a proposed 12-month moratorium on commercial battery storage facilities, with a formal vote and public hearing scheduled for May 19. Community & Economic Development Director James Henderson presented the proposal, describing it as a response to growing interest in large-scale battery energy storage systems in the region… The May 19 North Bend City Council meeting is open to the public. A public hearing on the moratorium ordinance is expected the same night.”

title: Desert Rainbow Mega Solar Farm Targets 5,000 Acres In Goodyear
snippet: “A global energy company is looking to cover a big slice of southern Goodyear with panels and batteries, filing plans for a roughly 5,011-acre solar and storage complex that would put out about 550 megawatts of solar power and store another 550 megawatts. The project, called Desert Rainbow, is slated for Rainbow Valley and is pitched as producing enough carbon-free electricity to serve the equivalent of roughly 165,000 homes. At that scale, it would rank among the largest utility-scale solar footprints proposed in Arizona in recent years… City records show a special-use permit application, Case No. P25-00259, submitted by Desert Rainbow LLC for roughly 5,011 acres south of Queen Creek Road and east of Waterman Wash in Rainbow Valley… AES has penciled in development and permitting through 2029, with construction expected to run from 2029 to 2032 and operations following that window.”

title: Planning Commission Approves Acreage Cap for Solar Panels – Noozhawk
snippet: “The Santa Barbara County Planning Commission decided to cap the number of acres available for solar panel use to 16,000 acres and streamline the building process for new installations. The commission approved the countywide cap in a 3-1 vote on Wednesday during a discussion about where solar panels could be built, and which projects would be exempted from permitting… The 16,000-acre cap chosen by the commission will limit solar panels on agricultural land to 2% of the county’s available acreage.”

title: As PJM Reopens Interconnection Queue, Experts Warn Damage to Maryland’s Clean Energy Plans Is Already Done
snippet: “For the first time in four years, regional grid operator PJM Interconnection is adding new generation projects to its interconnection queue. title: Move over, sheep. Cattle are grazing solar sites, too, and that’s good news for Virginia.
snippet: “Farmers are finding that cattle also thrive among solar panels — and they will get their chance to prove it in Virginia. The market for beef is vastly bigger than the market for lamb, so persuading farmers they should diversify into sheep as well as solar is a tall order. But if cattle prove as compatible with solar as sheep are, there will be vastly more opportunities for both farmers and the solar industry. Given the dire economic situation facing small farms in Virginia today, “cattle-voltaics” could offer a lifeline for rural communities.”

title: Confusing ballot wording may have tipped Ohio vote on renewables ban
snippet: “Exit polling suggests Richland County would have elected to repeal its wind and solar ban, if not for residents who mistakenly voted in favor of the restriction. SB 52 states that for any referendum against a county’s ban, its commissioners’ resolution needs a majority vote in favor to go into effect So, a ​“yes” vote means someone opposes the referendum effort to overturn the ban, while a ​“no” vote means the person backs the campaign to get rid of the ban. “It’s confusing,” County Commissioner Cliff Mears told Canary Media.”

title: Appeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suit
snippet: “The three-judge Michigan Court of Appeals panel ruled that state regulators followed proper legal processes when they set rules to carry out a controversial 2023 law that allows the Michigan Public Service Commission to approve large wind, solar and battery projects over local objections. But judges ruled the commission interpreted certain aspects of that law in ways that improperly limited local power.It wasn’t immediately clear how the split ruling could affect the multiple renewable energy development proposals currently awaiting state approval. However, state officials seemed to see it as a victory.”

title: Gas power leapfrogs wind for first time in 10 years in Texas’ grid connection queue
snippet: “For the last six months, the volume of gas generation in the Texas grid’s interconnection queue — the yearslong waiting list for electric generators wanting to connect to the grid — has surpassed wind.It’s the first time since January 2016 that gas has overtaken wind in the queue, a shift that reflects the policy and economic headwinds facing the wind industry and data centers favoring gas power as they seek to cash in on the artificial intelligence boom. ”

title: Amazon Invests in 700 MW of New Carbon-Free Energy Projects to Power Future Data Centers in Nevada
snippet: “Amazon announced that it is investing in 700 MW of new carbon-free energy projects in Nevada to power its future data center operations in the area. title: Massive Utah data center complex ‘can’ be powered by renewables, O’Leary says — after state insisted ‘100%’ natural gas “The first time a hyperscale data center project in Utah was discussed in public , a state official was explicit
snippet: It would be powered “100% off the Ruby Pipeline,” the natural gas line that crosses the area. The first phase of the project is expected to consume 3 gigawatts of electricity. By comparison, the entire state of Utah currently uses about 4 gigawatts. At full build, the data complex is expected to use 9 gigawatts, project boosters have said. title: CT Senate passes major solar energy legislation
snippet: The Connecticut Senate passed House Bill 5340 on the last day of the 2026 legislative session. The bill extends solar incentive programs for residential, commercial, and community solar through 2035, expedites permitting for plug-in solar panels, creates a statewide agrivoltaics program, and establishes a pilot for low- or no-cost solar in 100 environmental justice homes. It also imposes a one-year moratorium on large-scale solar in East Windsor and Enfield. Democratic Rep. Jonathan Steinberg stated, “This bill modernizes Connecticut’s renewable energy programs by introducing measures meant to make them more reliable, consistent, and flexible to economic fluctuations.” The bill now heads to Gov. Ned Lamont.

title: Warren County commissioners uphold current solar farm ordinance
snippet: Warren County commissioners unanimously voted to reject proposed text amendments to the solar farm ordinance that would have allowed larger projects beyond the current 150-acre cap. The decision upholds the 2022 ordinance and blocks amendments sought by TED Renewables for the Horizon Solar project (160 MW combined on ~900 acres across two sites). Planning Director Mark Bloomer warned the changes would “open the door to solar farms of any size.” The meeting drew more than 40 speakers and lasted over four hours.

title: Clean energy groups push back on North Carolina regulators' solar energy pause
snippet: The Southern Environmental Law Center filed a motion asking the North Carolina Utilities Commission to reconsider its order pausing Duke Energy’s 2026 solar procurement (roughly 770 MW). The unusual order was issued solely by Commission Chair William Brawley without a public hearing. SELC called the pause “arbitrary and capricious” and warned that delaying the “missing megawatts” threatens reliability and affordability.

title: No vote yet on large-scale Southern Michigan solar project after months of opposition
snippet: The Fayette Township Planning Commission postponed any decision on a special use permit for Ranger Power’s Heartwood Solar II project (approximately 1,000 acres of panels on 1,400 acres east of Jonesville). The hearing on May 7 drew strong resident opposition. Resident Steve Oleszkowicz stated, “One out of every four acres of productive, agricultural land in this township gone, converted, industrialized. That is not incremental change. That is not balanced development. That is a transformation of this community.” Ranger Power’s Brady Friss noted, “Load is growing in Michigan and solar is currently the most affordable way to meet that demand.” No new meeting date was set.

title: Appeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suit
snippet: A Michigan Court of Appeals panel upheld most of the Michigan Public Service Commission’s rules implementing Public Act 233 but sided with local governments on two key points: the 30-day local approval clock starts after a meeting with developers (not when offered), and any local unit touching project land qualifies as an “affected local unit” eligible for developer payments. The ruling largely affirms the state’s ability to approve large solar, wind, and battery projects over local objections when towns lack compatible ordinances. PSC spokesperson Matt Helms said the decision “largely affirms the Commission’s approach.”

title: Farmers push back on proposed solar farm that could convert 1,500 acres of northern Michigan farmland
snippet: Farmers in Wexford and Grand Traverse counties are opposing Ranger Power’s (via subsidiary Shipyard Solar) early-stage proposal for a large solar array on nearly 1,500 acres of prime agricultural land. The project has not yet filed formal applications. Farmer James Cade stated, “You’re taking valuable farm ground, that is some of the best ground in the state of Michigan to raise vegetables that are feeding people.” Ranger Power’s Ryan Wardin said developments are built on “voluntary partnerships with local landowners who see solar as a benefit to their farming operations.” A public meeting is scheduled for May 18.

title: State Land Office awards lease to proposed wind farm near Corona
snippet: The New Mexico State Land Office awarded a lease to Southwestern Power Group for a 212 MW portion of the 1,100 MW Cedarvale Wind Project in Torrance County (8 miles northwest of Corona). The full project includes 243 turbines on 10,160 acres of state trust land and 51,149 acres of private land. It is projected to generate $98.9 million in school revenue over the lease lifetime. Construction is expected to begin in 2027 with operations in 2029. Land Commissioner Stephanie Garcia Richard stated, “Projects like this one are job creators in our rural communities and create long-lasting revenue streams for our public schools and other institutions.”

title: No decision yet on proposed battery storage sites in Huntington Town
snippet: The Huntington Town Board has not yet decided on two proposed battery energy storage facilities (one by Key Capture Energy) in East Northport and near the Greenlawn substation. A public hearing was held Tuesday with significant resident concerns over fire safety and emergency response for volunteer fire departments. Town Councilman Dave Bennardo stated, “The amount of false alarms that you have with a battery storage facility, the protocols of waiting outside the gates until you can get management to open the doors, that's impossible right now for a volunteer firefighting crew.” The board plans to meet with fire officials before voting and is considering a six-month moratorium to study risks.

title: Resident-led campaign fails to reverse Ohio county’s ban on renewables
snippet: Voters in Richland County, Ohio, rejected a referendum (53%–47%) to overturn a ban on utility-scale solar and wind in 11 townships. The ban, passed in 2025, remains in place. Campaign leader Morgan Carroll said, “We made a commitment to stand up to our elected officials… That’s not nothing. That is everything.”

title: Dominion upbeat on offshore wind as cost estimate eases, sales rise
snippet: Dominion Energy reported that its Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind (CVOW) project (2.6 GW) is now 75% complete, with nine of 176 turbines installed and power generation underway since March 2026. The company lowered its capital cost estimate to $11.4 billion while maintaining a levelized cost of energy at $84/MWh. CEO Robert Blue said, “With no change to either LCOE or customer bill impacts, CVOW remains one of the most affordable sources of energy for our customers.” The project is expected to generate approximately $5 billion in fuel savings over its first 10 years.

title: Oshtemo launches ‘Community Conversations’ after battery storage prompts controversy
snippet: Oshtemo Township (Kalamazoo County) launched monthly “Community Conversations” (first one Thursday, May 7) to give residents more informal input opportunities after controversy over a proposed 260 MW battery storage facility by NewEdge Power in 2024. The project was blocked by a township moratorium. Supervisor Cheri Bell said the sessions are “meant to be welcoming, unhurried and focused on listening… We want people to feel comfortable asking questions, sharing ideas and helping shape Oshtemo’s future.”

title: Emery County Commission begins drafting ordinance to prohibit future utility-scale solar development
snippet: The Emery County Commission voted 2-1 on May 6, 2026, to begin drafting an ordinance prohibiting new utility-scale solar projects in the county. The move follows strong public opposition to a proposed solar project at Poison Springs Bench/Drunkard’s Wash. Commissioner Keven Jensen stated, “I think the strongest tool that we have to stop this project is the power of the ordinance.” Commissioner Jordan Leonard added, “I think at this point no more utility-scale solar… the public has spoken on that.”

title: Goshen adopts six-month solar energy moratorium
snippet: The Goshen Town Board passed a six-month moratorium on new solar farms at its April 23 meeting (4-0 vote). The moratorium does not affect projects already undergoing review. Councilman George Lyons stated, “We are not against solar farms. They are getting out of control. We want to take a closer look at it.” Councilman Richard Florio added, “We’re not looking to ban solar. We just want to make sure it’s done right.” Councilman Philip Canterino noted that solar farms were intended to be capped at five megawatts but projects were combining multiple parcels for higher output.

title: Baldwin County seeks legal clarity on solar moratorium amid Stockton project concerns
snippet: The Baldwin County Commission approved a resolution asking the Alabama attorney general for a formal opinion on whether it can impose a temporary moratorium on utility-scale solar developments in un-zoned, unincorporated areas (especially flood-prone zones). The request is driven by concerns over a proposed utility-scale solar farm on roughly 4,500 acres near the unincorporated community of Stockton by Silicon Ranch Corp. The nonprofit Friends of the Tensaw River has called for a one-year pause on permits to study environmental risks tied to the Tensaw River and its delta.

title: Marshall County approves ordinances restricting solar farms and prohibiting carbon capture
snippet: Marshall County commissioners gave final approval (2-0 vote) to the Farm Scale Solar Energy System ordinance, which strictly prohibits large-scale solar panel farms spanning multiple properties and acres across any zoning district. It allows private-use solar up to five acres per parcel. The board also approved a cleanup ordinance permanently prohibiting carbon capture projects.

title: West Lafayette City Council approves solar power restrictions
snippet: The West Lafayette City Council approved (5-1 vote) an ordinance limiting new commercial solar developments to 400 acres per project, with at least one mile between projects and 500 feet from neighboring property lines. Total commercial solar in the county is capped at 6,000 acres. District 5 Rep. Kathy Parker stated, “It’s better than nothing, right? … If nobody comes for a whole year, then maybe we reexamine … It’s a starting place.”

title: Public hearing process for Liberty Farms Solar continues in Richland Township
snippet: Consumers Energy’s proposed Liberty Farms Solar project (1,200 acres, 220 MW, enough to power over 50,000 homes) is moving through the public hearing process in Richland Township. A third meeting is scheduled for May 27 at Gull Lake Middle School at 6:30 p.m. Township Supervisor Bear Priest stated, “Those seven members who live here, work here in our community… They need to hear from our residents.” Resident Richard Jamieson (living 170 yards from the site) said, “There’s nothing good about it at all… It’s gonna kill the value of property around here. This is rich land.”

title: Developer of Watsonville battery storage project pivots to state permitting route
snippet: New Leaf Energy is withdrawing its county application for the Seahawk Energy Storage project (160-unit BESS on 16 acres at 90 Minto Road in unincorporated Watsonville) and will instead use the California Energy Commission’s “Opt-in” process under AB 205 to meet CAISO deadlines. Project lead Max Christian stated that the county’s draft ordinance amendments “would likely prevent the necessary upstream procurement commitments.” The company still supports the core draft ordinance, calling it “one of the — if not the — strongest battery storage ordinances in the country.”

title: Clay Town Board to hold public hearing on new rules for battery storage projects
snippet: The Town of Clay held a public hearing (May 4, 2026) at 7:47 p.m. at Town Hall on proposed Local Law No. 4 of 2026 to establish guidelines for battery energy storage systems (BESS). The hearing follows resident concerns over potential projects on Long Branch Road, Wetzel Road and Goguen Drive. Proposed rules include tighter site-location limits, updated fire-safety and emergency-response requirements, noise and screening standards, clearer decommissioning rules, and alignment with updated state fire codes. Deputy Supervisor Joe Bick stated, “The hearing will stay open until we get the law right.” No vote will be taken; the hearing will remain open until the law is finalized.

title: GE Vernova challenges Vineyard Wind’s claims of harm if it leaves project
snippet: GE Vernova filed an emergency motion to reconsider a preliminary injunction barring it from exiting its contract with Vineyard Wind 1, arguing that the project’s commercial operation date (achieved April 24, 2026) and activation of power purchase agreements undermine claims of irreparable harm. Vineyard Wind (806 MW, 62 turbines) is currently generating at less than half capacity. Attorney Michael Scheinkman for Vineyard Wind stated, “This is an 806-megawatt project that’s generating output at less than half of that, which is not sufficient to generate commercially viable levels of electricity.” The next court hearing is scheduled for May 12.

title: Tippecanoe County adopts revised solar ordinance, lifting 11-month moratorium
snippet: Tippecanoe County commissioners unanimously approved a new large-scale solar ordinance on May 4, 2026, ending an 11-month moratorium. The ordinance caps individual commercial solar developments at 400 acres, increases setbacks to 500 feet from adjacent property lines, and limits total commercial solar development in the county to 6,000 acres. Commissioner Dave Byers stated, “As the resident farmer up here, ... don’t think I don’t cringe every time we agree to a 40-acre development or an 80-acre development. I know that’s good ground disappearing. I was against this from the beginning. I see this as a compromise.” The changes were prompted by the proposed 1,700-acre Rainbow Trout Solar project.

title: Meridian Township Board considers creating data center and battery storage facility moratorium
snippet: The Meridian Township Board is considering a moratorium on data centers and battery storage facilities to allow time to develop protective guidelines. Supervisor Scott Hendrickson stated, “The reason why I was looking to chat about moratoriums, specifically, is because what I feel like we have seen across the state is a propensity to buy up property, eliminate the existing use and convert into a data center or a battery storage facility.” Community Planning Director Tim Schmitt noted no current proposals exist in the township. A decision could come at the May 7 meeting.

title: Two-year moratorium on commercial alternative energy projects in Grand County continues until June 2027
snippet: The Grand County Board of County Commissioners voted 3-0 to extend a two-year moratorium on commercial solar, wind, battery, and energy storage projects (those exceeding 25 kW) until June 24, 2027. The moratorium, which began June 24, 2025, gives the county time to update its master plan and draft regulations. Community Development Director Kristen Manguso stated, “We have less than 30 percent of developable area… The land is valuable here, and it is more valuable for development than it is for solar farms.” The measure does not affect residential solar.

title: Southold Town Board extends BESS moratorium to 2027 amid safety and zoning debate
snippet: The Southold Town Board unanimously approved a 12-month extension of its moratorium on battery energy storage systems through April 2027. The moratorium, first enacted in 2023, gives the town time to update zoning, reconvene the BESS Task Force, and address fire safety concerns following a proposed 60 MW lithium-ion BESS by Key Capture Energy on Oregon Road in Cutchogue. Supervisor Al Krupski stated, “The recommended 12-month extension of the moratorium would be time to complete and accomplish the steps recommended by the state in their new BESS guidance document.” Kevin O’Mara of Friends of Oregon Road said, “The town has spent millions of dollars preserving the agricultural land around this site, which would become unusable if there were a fire because of the contamination of these heavy metals.”

title: AG rules against Southwick’s temporary moratorium on solar arrays and BESS systems
snippet: The Massachusetts Attorney General’s office disapproved Southwick’s temporary moratorium on large-scale solar arrays and battery energy storage systems, ruling it lacked sufficient public health, safety, or welfare justification and violated state law prohibiting unreasonable regulation of solar installations. The moratorium had been approved at a Special Town Meeting in mid-January 2026 and was set to last until May 31, 2027. Town Planner Alvin Allen stated, “If a company puts in an application, we’d have to move forward through our current process and hear it within 60 days.” Planning Board Chair Jessica Thornton said the ruling “was not unexpected” and that the town is awaiting a state template for zoning bylaws.

title: House OKs solar bill; GOP targets impact on public benefits charge
snippet: The Connecticut House passed House Bill 5340 on May 1, 2026, reauthorizing the state’s residential, commercial, and community solar programs through 2035 with an $85 million annual budget target. The bill includes expedited permitting for plug-in solar panels, a one-year moratorium on new grid-scale solar in East Windsor and Enfield, and exemptions for solar paired with battery storage. Lori Brown of the Connecticut League of Conservation Voters said, “If they don’t pass a solar bill this session to extend the programs, it would be an epic failure on the part of a legislature.” State Sen. Ryan Fazio, R-Greenwich, called it “unconscionable policy… extraordinarily expensive.”

title: Virginia’s new law blocks counties from banning solar
snippet: Gov. Abigail Spanberger signed legislation that voids community-wide bans on solar fields and requires localities to submit written reasons when denying permits. The law takes effect July 1. Two-thirds of Virginia counties currently ban or heavily restrict solar. Evan Vaughan of the Mid-Atlantic Renewable Energy Coalition said, “Localities still are in the driver’s seat here. They can still deny every project from now until the end of time if they want.”

title: Regulators hit pause on Duke Energy solar expansion
snippet: The North Carolina Utilities Commission ordered Duke Energy to pause its 2026 solar procurement process (approximately 770 MW) until the Commission completes review of the company’s updated Carbon Plan. Decisions on new solar could be delayed until late 2026 or later. Will Scott of the Environmental Defense Fund stated, “Delaying new solar procurements will only hurt households and the businesses that rely on North Carolina’s pro-business environment.”

title: Wilson looking to pass battery energy storage law
snippet: “Wilson is set to vote on a new local law limiting where certain battery energy storage systems can operate in the village. The law would limit Tier 2 battery storage systems in industrial-zoned areas, which for the village would be its southern parcels. Tier 1 storage systems, which can store up to 600 kWh, are allowed in all areas of the village, given their usage for rooftop solar systems. The language is based on the model law for battery storage that NYSERDA released, with Village Mayor Gregory Marin saying it is very similar to the Village of Lancaster’s law… The Niagara County Planning Board gave its support to this law at its April meeting, with the village board planning to vote on the law at its May 21 meeting. Wilson is among many Niagara County municipalities looking to establish such regulations, with others including Cambria, Newfane, Pendleton, and Porter.”

title: Headline
snippet: “ANDOVER — The Ashtabula County Board of Commissioners spoke to local residents Wednesday at the Andover Public Library… Commissioner J.P. Ducro spoke about potential wind and solar power projects in the county. ‘The position of the commissioners has really been wanting to defer to local control,’ he said… He said the commissioners are aware some people have been approached about land leases. ‘Nothing has really even come to our desk at this point and time for us to weigh in on officially,’ Ducro said. Under Ohio Senate Bill 52, county commissioners have the power to give a thumbs up or down to economically significant wind and solar projects, which generate between five and 50 megawatts, he said. Ducro said the commissioners are working with the county building department to map out where they would permit or not permit wind and solar projects. He said the plan is to detail and update the map with each township wants them, do not want them or wants to restrict them.‘That process is probably going to take some time,’ Ducro said. ‘We imagine that that’s going to probably take us through the year 2026.’”

title: [Opinion] No time for timidity on clean energy
snippet: “Massachusetts has long been a leader in advancing smart, effective energy and environmental policies that reduce pollution, protect public health, create jobs, and lower costs for consumers over time. But lately, too many policymakers in Massachusetts are staying silent or even backtracking on proven strategies. Policymakers are failing to push back as fossil fuel interests try to distract from the harm they cause to our environment, public health, and runaway energy costs. title: Cerro Gordo County officials clash with state over solar development
snippet: “The Iowa Utilities Commission has issued a final order advancing review of a proposed large-scale solar project in Cerro Gordo County, setting up a clash with local officials who enacted a moratorium and subsequent ban on such developments. In a decision filed Monday, April 27, the commission required additional information from River City Energy LLC as part of its application for a generating certificate for a 500-megawatt solar facility planned on roughly 2,250 acres in the county. The state’s action comes after Cerro Gordo County supervisors already moved to halt such projects locally. Following months of public meetings and debate, the board approved an ordinance amendment banning new utility-scale wind, solar and battery storage developments in unincorporated areas, replacing a temporary moratorium first adopted to study land-use impacts.”

title: [Opinion] Proposed Evergy electric transmission line would only hurt Kansas
snippet: “As a Kansas rancher, I am writing to sound the alarm on Evergy’s proposed 133-mile, 345 kilovolt Buffalo Flats transmission line. This project, currently before the Kansas Corporation Commission, represents a profound threat to our state’s heritage, economy and environment. First, the Buffalo Flats project stinks of an insider deal. Unlike most major infrastructure, this $500 million project was not put out for public bid. On other Kansas projects, competitive bidding for transmission lines has realized savings of 40%. By bypassing this process, Kansas ratepayers are potentially left on the hook for hundreds of millions in unnecessary costs.”

title: York County Commissioners approve zoning for solar farm, creates ‘tough environment’ for project
snippet: “The York County Commissioners voted 3-2 to approve the zoning regulations surrounding a proposed 3,000-acre solar farm. Dozens of community members came out to make their voices heard one last time, filling every seat in the room and pleading with commissioners. ‘I moved out to York County to be surrounded by farm land, not an industrial solar park,’ said Kirkland Doht, a York County resident. The zoning regulations in place include a half-mile setback from any homeowner who is not a willing partner and from platted subdivisions. Other setbacks include 330 feet from cemeteries and 660 feet from state recreational areas. Commissioners decided to adjust the setback from churches and schools, which was previously set to 660 feet and is now a half-mile.”

On Wednesday, the Wichita-Sedgwick County Metropolitan Area Planning Commission will consider a request from Galena Solar Project, LLC to rezone 1,490 acres from about 119th Street West to a little east of South Ridge Road and from West 47th Street South to West 63rd Street South. This is just southwest of Wichita.”

title: NY PSC to Hold Public Statement Hearing to Discuss Two Rivers Solar Farm
snippet: “The New York Public Service Commission will hold a virtual public statement hearing to discuss Two Rivers Solar Farm LLC’s proposed 200-megawatt solar electric generating facility in St. Lawrence County. The facility would interconnect to the power grid via the New York Power Authority’s Haverstock – Willis E 345 kilovolt transmission line. The company plans to build the project on land leased and/or purchased from owners of private property within an area of approximately 3,168 acres. The project has the potential to power approximately 56,000 homes. The company is seeking a lightened regulatory regime as it intends to operate the project as a competitive wholesale generator. Comments are due by May 6. 25-E-0616 ”

title: Guilderland sets four hearings inspired by updated comprehensive plan
snippet: “On April 21, the board agreed to set public hearings for four proposed local laws… A moratorium on battery energy storage systems, known as BESS… During the April 21 public comment period, Robyn Gray, who chairs the Guilderland Coalition for Responsible Growth, told the board that residents had been receiving solicitation letters from energy developers offering ‘anywhere between ten- and thirty-thousand dollars a year rent’ to install shipping-container-sized batteries on their properties… Supervisor Peter Barber told Gray the town was already moving on the issue, proposing to enact a temporary ban on BESS installations. Later in the meeting, the board set a May 19 public hearing on Barber’s proposed four-month moratorium.”

title: Town of Oyster Bay approves sixth battery energy system storage moratorium
snippet: “The Town of Oyster Bay board voted unanimously to extend its moratorium on battery energy storage systems for another 12 months, citing ongoing safety concerns and continued public opposition during a recent meeting. The resolution passed with six votes in favor and none opposed as part of a broader action calendar approved by the town board during their regular April 14 meeting. It is another win for the anti-battery storage movement in the Glen Head community, which rallied around opposition to the Oyster Shore Energy Storage, a now-cancelled project by Jupiter Power in Glen Head which the moratorium was initially created to prevent.”

title: Dearborn County plan commission votes against creating a solar farm advisory committee
snippet: “The Dearborn County Plan Commission voted unanimously 8-0 against forming an advisory committee to amend commercial ordinances regarding solar, battery energy storage systems and data centers. The vote took place Monday evening, during the commission's first meeting since a 12-month moratorium on such developments was enacted in February. Elected officials said there will still be opportunities for public input throughout the ordinance process… It will now be up to the county commissioners to decide if a review committee is formed at that level, as recommended by the Plan Commission. The first in a series of informational workshops will be held May 20, where officials said there will be more opportunities for public input.”

title: CT public benefits charge cut; electricity rates to fall by nearly 15%
snippet: “Electricity rates in Connecticut will decline by nearly 15% in May, after state regulators on Wednesday approved changes that will all but remove the public benefits charge from customer bills. The Public Utilities Regulatory Authority voted unanimously in favor of the rate adjustments, which largely affect the costs borne by utilities for various state-mandated policy programs such as clean energy procurements and benefits for low-income customers.”

title: House defeats advanced nuclear proposal from Senate
snippet: “The House of Representatives voted Thursday against a bill that sought to allow utilities to build and own advanced nuclear reactors. Senate Bill 447 was defeated on the consent calendar by voice vote. From prime sponsor Sen. Kevin Avard, a Nashua Republican, the proposal would have allowed utilities to own ‘advanced’ nuclear reactors up to 300 megawatts in capacity, about one quarter the size of New Hampshire’s only active nuclear reactor in Seabrook.”

title: Southold Town Board extends BESS moratorium to 2027 amid safety, zoning debate
snippet: “Southold Town Board unanimously approved a 12-month extension of its moratorium on Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) facilities on Tuesday — buying time to draft and vote on local legislation governing their use. The latest extension keeps the moratorium in place through April 2027. The law was first enacted by the Town Board on Jan. 31, 2023, with an effective date of April 11, 2023, following public scrutiny of a proposed BESS facility by Key Capture Energy on Oregon Road in Cutchogue the previous year… Town officials said the additional year will allow them to reconvene the town’s BESS Task Force, gather public input and craft regulations aligned with new state guidance.”

title: Baldwin County residents push for solar moratorium as legal review continues
snippet: “A group of North Baldwin County residents is asking commissioners to temporarily halt large-scale solar development, citing environmental concerns and a lack of independent oversight. Megan Fowler spoke today on behalf of Friends of the Tensaw River, a newly formed grassroots organization, who made the request during Tuesday’s Baldwin County Commission meeting. The group is calling for a temporary moratorium on new utility-scale solar projects - potentially lasting up to 180 days - to give the county time to study environmental impacts, hire an independent engineering firm, and consider new regulations.”

title: Illinois Court Orders County to Issue Permits for Six Solar Energy Projects
snippet: “A Will County circuit court ordered the county board to issue permits for six solar energy projects in one of the first decisions applying last month’s landmark Equity Solar appellate ruling. Several lawsuits are pending throughout Illinois in which solar energy developers are challenging county boards that have denied siting permits for solar energy facilities… In Channahon McKinley Woods Solar, the Will County court ruled on six consolidated cases brought by solar energy developers against the county. The developers challenged the county’s refusal to issue special use permits for proposed solar facilities despite the proposed facilities meeting all statutory requirements. The court granted summary judgment in all cases, ordering the county to approve ordinances granting special use permits for all plaintiffs by April 17.”
