title: Neighbors, Zeeland Township take steps to oppose proposed solar farm in Ottawa County
snippet: “Neighbors in Zeeland and Jamestown townships are intensifying their opposition to a proposed solar farm, taking steps to become formal participants in the state review process. Earlier this month, project developer RWE Americas, a subsidiary of RWE, filed an application with the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) to construct the 200-megawatt Silver Maple Solar Farm, bypassing local permitting efforts. The proposed facility would cover 1,900 acres in Zeeland and Jamestown townships and produce enough energy to power more than 34,000 homes. On Friday, nearly 100 residents gathered at the Ottawa Executive Airport in Zeeland Township for a workshop guiding them through how to petition to intervene in the state’s regulatory process — a step that allows certain parties to submit information and have a voice during hearings.”

title: Renewable ordinance headed to final stages
snippet: “One way or the other, the months-long saga over a proposed renewable energy ordinance for Wapello County may be nearing its conclusion. The board of supervisors set May 5 as the public hearing for the first reading of a revised ordinance that was reworked following a mediation session two months ago between Darren Batterson, Bryan Ziegler and Des Moines attorney Eric Updegraff. The third-floor McGiverin Courtroom was filled just for the date-setting hearing, with a handful of county residents speaking against the Dusty Fox Wind project. Because of strict setbacks contained in the revised ordinance, the Dusty Fox project — despite nearly 20,000 acres of land signed up for wind turbine leases — may be in jeopardy… It takes only one supervisor to vote no for the ordinance to fail, since supervisor Carrie Teninty has abstained due to a potential conflict of interest after her family signed a lease three years ago.”

title: OPPD halts $610M York County solar project amid zoning delays
snippet: “Omaha Public Power District (OPPD) has allowed its Generator Interconnection Agreement for the proposed K-Junction solar project near McCool Junction to expire, pausing the project for now. The utility said the decision was made because of ongoing uncertainty around York County’s proposed solar zoning regulations. The project, which could have delivered up to 310 megawatts of solar power and generated more than $610 million in economic development and $30 million in local tax revenue, cannot move forward until new interconnection milestones are approved. A public hearing on the county’s proposed utility-scale solar zoning regulations is scheduled for April 27 at the York County Courthouse. Residents can attend to learn more or share input on the future of solar development in the area.”

title: Snoqualmie neighbors rally against battery storage facility as permit application looms
snippet: “Hundreds of Snoqualmie residents marched through their neighborhood Sunday — one day before the developer behind a proposed lithium-ion battery storage facility is expected to file a permit application with King County… The proposed 45-acre facility would be located on parcels immediately south of the park. Residents carried signs reading ‘Don't BESS With Us’ — BESS standing for Battery Energy Storage System — and chanted as they marched. Many brought their children, and some of those children took to the podium to address the crowd, echoing their parents' concerns about the project. The protest marked a rapid escalation of community opposition. Residents say they only learned about the project roughly a month ago, despite planning being underway since at least 2023.”

title: Appeal of Bayview Ridge battery energy storage facility set for June
snippet: “An appeal of a Skagit County special-use permit for a battery energy storage system on Bayview Ridge will be heard June 23 in Whatcom County Superior Court. The Stewards of Skagit have filed the appeal over the permit, which was issued in January 2025. In June 2025, the Skagit County Board of Commissioners denied an appeal of the permit by the Stewards of Skagit, setting up the appeal in court under the state's Land Use Petition Act. The Stewards of Skagit cite concerns regarding safety, and an improper approval process by the county hearing examiner. The battery energy storage system, or BESS, proposed by NextEra Energy would be on 22 acres of land on McFarland Road west of Burlington and connect to Puget Sound Energy's nearby Fredonia substation. The system would use lithium-ion batteries capable of storing up to 200 megawatt hours of electricity.”

title: Duke Energy’s proactive grid upgrades under fire from electric co-ops
snippet: “Duke Energy, the state’s predominant utility, is moving to proactively upgrade poles and wires to create room for prospective solar farms. Rather than making improvements pegged to specific projects and then charging solar developers for the full cost, as it did in the past, the company is now building in anticipation of future grid needs and spreading the costs among all customers…But one set of Duke customers isn’t happy. North Carolina’s electric member cooperatives, which buy most of their power wholesale from the utility, filed a complaint with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission in February over four grid projects. They argue that the cost of the upgrades — $57 million, in this case — should not be distributed evenly among all customers. Instead, they want solar developers to pay half the total cost. Many observers believe the protest is on shaky legal ground. Yet FERC is chaired by an appointee of President Donald Trump, who is known to attack renewable energy regardless of the law.”

title: 19 cities across Utah could be on clean energy sources, if their councils pass an ordinance
snippet: “Environmental groups are rallying people in 19 communities across Utah to contact their town and city councils, urging them to pass an ordinance saying they want to be on 100% net renewable energy…The Public Service Commission, the entity that regulates electricity rates in Utah, approved the Community Clean Energy Program last month. title: Sparked by AES battery development, residents ask Huntingburg for development moratorium
snippet: “Huntingburg resident Amanda Souders Harris urged the common council to enact a temporary moratorium on new development within the city’s extraterritorial jurisdiction (ETJ) until the Unified Development Ordinance can be updated to address large-scale projects that current regulations weren’t designed to handle… The request comes as the city grapples with an ongoing solar project by AES that includes a battery energy storage system. Part of the project is located within the city’s extraterritorial jurisdiction, but not the batteries… City Attorney Phil Schneider explained that moratoriums require going through the UDO amendment process, including referral to the planning commission, public hearings and passing an ordinance. State law limits moratoriums to one year, and the city would need to hire consultants to draft development plans or restrictions during that time.”

title: Texas sharpens attacks on solar power
snippet: “Texas lawmakers and regulators have unleashed blistering new attacks against solar this month, setting up a showdown that could curb renewable energy growth. State senators grilled solar advocates over whether Chinese-made solar panels could give the Communist Party of China the ability to meddle with Texas’s isolated electric grid. The Public Utility Commission of Texas sent a solar and battery project back to a state administrative court, telling El Paso Electric it should consider natural gas or other traditional generation. And state Attorney General Ken Paxton (R) said his office filed civil investigative demands with four companies as part of a new initiative to investigate “widespread” fraud among rooftop solar companies.”

title: Kansas county weighs moratorium on solar development
snippet: “The Jackson County, Kansas Planning Commission met at the county courthouse to deliberate on a recommendation for a formal moratorium of large-scale solar development. If approved by the Board of County Commissioners, the pause would effectively freeze new solar project applications while officials finalize a permanent regulatory framework. The process that has remained in legislative flux since December 2024. The move follows years of escalating debate over the Jeffrey Solar project, a 5,000-acre, 500 MW installation proposed by NextEra Energy Resources.”

title: Oshtemo Township hears from experts on BESS ordinance options under state law
snippet: Oshtemo Township (Kalamazoo County) held a meeting with experts from EGLE, MPSC and the University of Michigan to discuss drafting a local ordinance for battery energy storage systems (BESS) under Michigan's Public Act 233. A proposed BESS by NewEdge Renewable Power near Van Kal Avenue on the Oshtemo-Almena border triggered a one-year moratorium (approved end of 2025 and potentially extendable). All seven township officials and over 350 families oppose the project; more than 1,500 residents are in the "Say No to Oshtemo BESS" Facebook group with over 1,000 petition signatures. Resident Katie Schneider stated, "I'm a business owner, a farmer and a parent here in Oshtemo Township. I can tell you there are over 350 families with signs in their yard against this project." Experts noted fire/explosion risk but said lithium-ion batteries are "getting increasingly safe without injury" and that a "workable ordinance" must sit in the "sweet spot" above the state minimum to prevent developers from bypassing to the MPSC. No vote was taken; the planning commission will continue drafting with multiple future public-comment meetings.

title: Proposed wind turbines causing a storm in rural AR
snippet: A proposed 94-turbine wind farm in Crittenden County has sparked strong resident opposition ahead of a possible final vote this month on the first phase. The project would bring an estimated $26 million over 15 years, mostly to school districts like Earle, but residents cite lack of notice, property-value impacts, noise, fire-safety concerns (local departments lack equipment for turbine fires), and long-term effects over 30–40 years. Resident Carrie Fraley said, "These are rights that we have where we live, where we're raising our family, where we want to retire, and I'm concerned about the property value for everyone out here." Volunteer firefighter Scott Fraley added, "The big issue is… our little fire department does not have the means to barely put out a grass fire." Residents are circulating a petition for a moratorium and independent study; the Quorum Court is hiring a consultant on setbacks and impacts. The developer's attorney noted the study towers have operated for 10 years with "no incidents."

title: Solar project moratorium may be coming in Jackson County
snippet: Jackson County leaders are considering a moratorium on solar projects after years of discussion around NextEra Energy Resources' proposed 500 MW Jeffrey Solar project west of Holton that could use up to 2,000 acres. The project is projected to generate $136 million in new tax revenue over its lifespan, including $24.5 million in the first decade. NextEra representative Laura Gies stated, "Jeffrey Solar will contribute approximately $24.5 million to the area in the first decade of operations and is projected to generate a total of $136 million in new tax revenue over its lifespan. This funding can help support schools, roads and other vital public services." The Jackson County Planning Commission was scheduled to discuss and possibly recommend the moratorium at a public meeting on April 9, 2026 (7–8 p.m. at Memorial Hall in Holton).

title: Tiny Boulevard Town Goes Big, Demands $7M And Safety Overhaul From Starlight Solar
snippet: The tiny town of Boulevard (San Diego County, pop. ~400) is demanding a $7 million community benefits agreement and major safety changes from Starlight Solar's proposed project on 588 acres along Jewel Valley Road. The development would pair up to 100 MW of solar with a 217.4 MW battery energy storage system. Residents and neighborhood groups want the battery component moved farther back, a second evacuation route, detailed fire plans, water-use assurances, larger setbacks, and funding to refurbish the Backcountry Resource Center and build a playground. They cite precedent from the Jacumba Valley Ranch project ($4 million community fund). The project faces county Planning Commission review on May 8; a town hall is scheduled for April 16.

title: TVA CEO Don Moul to retire July 1
snippet: Tennessee Valley Authority CEO Don Moul, who has served as TVA's fourth CEO since April 9, 2025, plans to retire July 1, informing the board of his decision on April 3. TVA is the nation's largest public utility and supplies power to the Glasgow Electric Plant Board. "The Board appreciates Don's service to TVA, its employees and the people of the Tennessee Valley region," Director Mitch Graves said. "Under his leadership, TVA has had strong operational and financial performance delivering reliable, affordable, American energy that helps communities across our seven states prosper." Moul's decision comes after President Donald Trump publicly criticized him and vowed to make his life "miserable." Trump has appointed four of the six board members who will pick the next CEO. In 2025, Moul drew roughly $6 million in direct compensation, but Trump is pressing the board to cap CEO pay at $500,000.

title: Solar power company appeals to south Alabama residents
snippet: 'We double crop': About 200 people attended a town hall Wednesday night at the civic center in Bay Minette where Silicon Ranch representatives addressed concerns about the Nashville-based company's proposed 4,500-acre solar farm in Stockton, with tensions boiling over late in the meeting into a verbal altercation that resulted in one woman being escorted out. The solar panels are expected to generate 260 megawatts and be operational by the end of 2028, and Silicon Ranch has a power purchase agreement with Alabama Power to offset energy demand from Meta's forthcoming $1.5 billion data center in Montgomery. "Your bill is not going up because of solar," said Reagan Farr, CEO of Silicon Ranch. "We're not sneaking around." Silicon Ranch officials said 2,500 acres of the site — largely wetlands and bordering areas — will be conserved, while solar panels on the remaining 2,000 acres will host a flock of sheep. "We double crop," Farr said. "We harvest sunlight to produce energy, and we sell grass-fed meat…It basically runs as an agricultural operation after it's built." About 75 residents protested outside the civic center before the event, joined by former U.S. Rep. Jerry Carl, who is running for Alabama's first congressional district. The article also notes developers are looking to build a nearly 3,000-acre solar farm further north in Baldwin County by Tensaw Solar, LLC.

title: Heelstone Renewable starts construction on 206MW US solar projects
snippet: Heelstone Renewable Energy, a Qualitas Energy company, has started construction on two solar photovoltaic projects in the US with a combined capacity of 206MW, supported by long-term corporate power purchase agreements with a US hyperscale data centre developer. The 104MW Alligator Creek Solar facility is located in Wheeler County, Georgia, and the 102MW Murch Solar project is in Van Buren County, Michigan. Heelstone expects both plants to reach commercial operation by the end of 2026. Alligator Creek reached financial close in December 2025 and Murch closed in March 2026. Heelstone appointed Pure Power Contractors for Alligator Creek and Greensol Renewables for Murch. "Backed by long-term PPAs and fully structured project financing, these projects reflect our ability to originate, finance and build high-quality utility-scale renewable assets," said Heelstone Renewable Energy CEO Mike Weich. "The start of construction on Alligator Creek and Murch marks another important step in scaling our platform across key US markets." The milestones follow Heelstone's recent completion of a $200m senior secured corporate credit facility.

title: Xcel Minnesota is building a first-of-its-kind virtual power plant
snippet: The Minnesota Public Utilities Commission last week approved Xcel Energy's plan to become the first utility in the nation to build and operate its own virtual power plant. Under the new program, known as Capacity*Connect, Xcel will spend up to $430 million to deploy up to 200 megawatts of batteries, in 1-megawatt to 3-megawatt increments, over the next two years. Almost every other virtual power plant program in the U.S. is organized around third-party companies — like solar and battery vendors or specialized "aggregators" — that tap into energy resources installed and owned by customers. Clean energy advocates, solar industry groups, and state agencies had raised concerns that Xcel's utility-owned model would crowd out competition. Shannon Anderson of Solar United Neighbors said: "The Minnesota story is part of a national trend… There are a number of filings coming, a lot of paperwork to evaluate and justify the program. This is just the beginning of the conversation from our perspective."

title: AES pulls out of San Diego area battery project after local opposition
snippet: AES Corp. withdrew its application to develop the Seguro battery system in Escondido, 30 miles from San Diego, after a barrage of local opposition. The company had intended to fill a former horse ranch with 320 megawatts of battery containers, which would have been one of the most powerful stand-alone energy storage facilities in the country and would have strengthened the Southern California grid late in the day when solar generation fades and home consumption surges. Residents decried adding large-scale power infrastructure so close to homes and 1,600 feet from Palomar Medical Center Escondido, especially given the multiday fires at other large batteries in the state. AES needed the hospital to sign off on running high-voltage wires through its property, which the hospital board voted down in 2024. AES said in a statement it would prioritize other development efforts but remained "committed to advancing projects that can provide the safe, reliable, and affordable power needed to strengthen the region's electric grid and generate meaningful economic benefits locally." JP Theberge, a board member of the Elfin Forest Harmony Grove Town Council who rallied resistance to the battery, framed his neighbors as a victorious David smiting a corporate Goliath. "The forces of greed are very powerful in this world. The only way to stop them is to be united and determined and forceful," he wrote in a post on the Stop Seguro Facebook group. "This was a great win and we should all be proud of our efforts."

title: New York State clears Cordelio's plan for 300-MW solar project
snippet: New York's Office of Renewable Energy Siting and Electric Transmission has issued a final siting permit to Cordelio Power for the Flat Creek Solar Project, an up to 300 MW facility spanning roughly 1,000 acres in the Towns of Canajoharie and Root in Montgomery County. Cordelio plans to build the project in two phases — an initial 200 MW followed by another 100 MW — with startup targeted for late 2028 or early 2029. Flat Creek is expected to generate approximately $10 million in direct payments to the host towns over the first 20 years, $16.6 million to the Canajoharie Central School District, and $9.8 million to Montgomery County. ORES issued the Cordelio approval alongside a separate siting permit for Liberty Renewables' 99 MW Agricola wind project in Cayuga County.

title: Solar farm opposition drives Stockton to historic zoning vote
snippet: The Baldwin County Commission on Tuesday certified a petition to put a zoning referendum before voters in unincorporated Stockton, likely on June 30, in response to outrage over Nashville-based Silicon Ranch's proposed $350 million, 2,000-acre solar farm near the community. The commission the same day rescinded a prior contract with Jacobs Engineering to oversee the solar farm application after residents raised concerns about the firm's ties to the solar industry. Organizers gathered 176 signatures within 24 hours — well above the roughly 124 required. "It's a citizen driven process," said Commissioner Jeb Ball, who represents the rural northern part of the county. Commissioner Matt McKenzie cautioned residents: "I want the people who are listening to be careful what you ask for. Once it gets zoned, it's a double edge sword." Meagan Fowler, representing the citizens group opposed to the project over concerns about runoff and damage to the Mobile-Tensaw Delta, said, "They do not want to become a town of industrial solar."

title: Silicon Ranch to meet with north Baldwin County residents over proposed solar farm
snippet: Silicon Ranch will meet with North Baldwin County residents at 6:30 p.m. Wednesday at the John F. Rhodes Civic Center in Bay Minette to discuss plans for its proposed large-scale solar farm near Stockton. The Baldwin County Commission on Tuesday passed a resolution requiring a special referendum to establish zoning in Planning District 3, and the probate court has 90 days to schedule the election. If voters reject the measure, current conditions remain; a yes vote triggers a 180-day moratorium on development within Planning District 3 while residents form a committee and work out zoning guidelines. The county has not yet received building applications from Silicon Ranch.

title: One-year moratorium on solar farms fails on Alabama Senate procedural rule
snippet: The Alabama Senate on Tuesday passed SB 354, sponsored by Sen. Greg Albritton, R-Atmore, which would prohibit new solar facilities in Mobile and Baldwin counties for one year, but failed to transmit it to the House because unanimous consent is required after the 26th legislative day and multiple senators objected. The bill passed 27-4 with two amendments — one narrowing the moratorium from statewide to just Mobile and Baldwin counties and requiring 30-day public hearing notice before construction, and another adding carbon dioxide storage facilities to the prohibition. The legislation was prompted by the controversy over Silicon Ranch's proposed 2,000-acre solar project on a 4,500-acre parcel in Stockton. "It's not stopping the industry. It is not restricting the industry. It is simply changing looking at a way to find another process that we could be more open," Albritton said.

title: Knox County Commissioners Move Forward on Solar Ordinance Revisions, Form Advisory Committee
snippet: Knox County Commissioners voted 2-1 to send proposed amendments to the county's 2020 solar energy ordinance to the Area Plan Commission for review. The amendments would establish a 300-foot setback between commercial solar energy systems and any existing residence or principal structure, and would replace current screening rules with stricter standards requiring opaque fencing and vegetative buffers. Commissioners also unanimously formed a new 12-member Energy Land Use Advisory Committee to continue revising the solar ordinance and begin developing regulations for data centers, wind energy, and battery storage, none of which are currently covered under county ordinances. "I think the ordinance has got great bones, but very weak in meat and so we need to put meat to it," Commissioner TJ Brink said.

title: Commissioners vote not to change solar ordinance
snippet: Posey County, Indiana residents gave their final input at a county commissioners meeting on proposed solar farm ordinance language that would keep the 500-foot setback from property lines rather than reducing it to the 300 feet sought by solar developers. Companies including ENGIE disagreed with the current wording. "I think the way that the ordinance is currently drafted and being sent back to the Area Planning Commission without modifications to the solar portion, is an impediment to investment and economic development in the county," said Ryan Economy. Commissioner Greg Newman explained the commissioners' rationale: "I personally believe that the changes will not prevent solar from coming into the county. And that's my own personal belief. We just tried to find a balance between those that would be affected, that didn't want to participate in the project, and those landowners that did."

title: Judge vacates permit for 810-acre Northern Waters Solar project in Cheboygan County
snippet: A judge has overturned Cheboygan County's approval of EDP Renewables' proposed 110 MW, 810-acre Northern Waters Solar Park in Grant Township, which would power the equivalent of more than 30,000 homes. Nineteen residents had asked the court to reverse the county's approval of the special use permit. Judge Gauthier ruled that because the application was for a special use permit, findings of fact on each section of the zoning ordinance must be made, even though the county argued its Compatible Renewable Energy Ordinance made large solar a permitted use by right. A few months after the original approval, the planning commission removed the CREO from its ordinance and placed a temporary moratorium on large-scale solar, but "the day after this court ruled in January, the board of commissioners rescinded that ordinance," the plaintiffs' attorney said. County Administrator Jeffrey Lawson said the county will respect the decision. "Just the community coming together to fight this. We were unwavering, relentless, and we knew that we were fighting for justice," said resident Sherry Long.

title: Oshtemo Township officials clarify status of energy systems ordinance and their process
snippet: The Oshtemo Township Planning Commission is continuing work Thursday on developing a zoning ordinance for battery energy storage system facilities, five months after the township board passed a one-year moratorium on such developments in November. The Planning Commission will hear from public sector and academic guest speakers, with additional scientific and technical experts expected to provide input on BESS safety provisions. In a news release Tuesday, township officials pushed back on public comments suggesting little work is happening on the ordinance or that it is being developed behind closed doors: "Those claims are not accurate. No ordinance drafting has occurred in closed session." NewEdge Renewable Power gave a presentation to the planning commission last August but no formal proposals have been submitted. A recall effort is currently targeting five board members, with organizers citing concerns over BESS safety and environmental risks.

title: Jackson Co Planning Commission to consider solar moratorium
snippet: The Jackson County Planning Commission is set to decide Thursday evening whether to recommend a temporary pause on large-scale solar development in the county. At a recent town hall, Commissioners Linda Gerhardt and Keith Kelly voiced opposition to large solar developments, while Commissioner Mark Pruett said he wouldn't support any project larger than 2,000 acres. County officials have been working on commercial solar regulations since December 2024. At the center of the debate is the proposed Jeffrey Solar project by NextEra Energy Resources, which would cover roughly 5,000 acres near Delia in southwestern Jackson County.

title: How much more wind energy might Wyoming see? It's complicated
snippet: A WyoFile analysis finds it's difficult to size up how much additional wind development is on the horizon for Wyoming, with no single clearinghouse tracking Wyoming-specific project information. The state currently has about 1,500 wind turbines with a generating capacity of approximately 3,700 MW, roughly 25% of total electrical generation capacity. Citing a court ruling, the Wyoming state lands board in February began invalidating two wind leases it had approved last year — one for the Pronghorn H2 Clean Energy Project in Converse County and one for the Sidewinder Clean Hydrogen Project in Niobrara County. Cheyenne area resident Wendy Volk recently helped launch a petition asking Wyoming officials to pause renewable energy permitting to conduct a "cumulative, landscape-level impact analysis." "We need to improve the process and transparency," Volk said. The piece also notes that entering election season, Republican U.S. House candidates including sitting Secretary of State Chuck Gray and Reid Rasner are campaigning on opposition to wind expansion.

title: Solar company submits wetlands permit application
snippet: York Run Solar, LLC has filed a joint application with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers for a wetlands permit, including a water quality certification submission to the New York State Office of Renewable Energy Siting and Electric Transmission, as it advances toward construction of a 90-megawatt solar facility on approximately 550 acres across the towns of Busti and Kiantone in Chautauqua County. The project received its ORES siting permit in April 2025. Construction is complicated by the fact that both towns have active moratoriums on commercial solar, wind, and battery energy storage systems — extended in June 2025 through May 31, 2026 — though those moratoriums do not apply to projects already permitted through ORES.

title: Will County commission says no in advisory vote on 6,100-acre solar complex
snippet: The Will County Board Planning and Zoning Commission voted 4-2 against Earthrise Energy’s Pride of the Prairie solar complex — a proposed 600+ MW facility spread across 6,100 acres on 96 farm properties in Manhattan, Green Garden, and Wilton townships — following a packed two-night public hearing that drew hundreds of residents. The vote is advisory; the project next went to the County Board Land Use and Development Committee, which on April 3 split 3-3 on Pride of the Prairie while advancing the company’s second project, Plum Valley Solar (2,250 acres), on a 4-2 vote. Both projects now head to the full county board for a final decision. “The problem we have with the Earthrise Pride of the Prairie is it’s just too darn big,” said Manhattan Township Supervisor James Walsh, who called it a “monster project.” Supporters included building trades unions, solar advocates, and farmers who stand to benefit from leasing their land.

title: Greene County Commission rejects battery storage site plan
snippet: The Greene County Commission voted 2-0 on April 6 to deny Alabama-based Sofos Power’s request to rezone 40 acres at 4873 N. Rocky Lane — just north of Springfield — to develop the Summit Resilient Power Project, a lithium-ion battery energy storage system. The commission rejected the plan despite changes the developer had made to address concerns, including reducing battery containers from 242 to 222 and adding stormwater infrastructure and fire hydrants. The county’s fire chief testified that any incident at the facility would require a Region D emergency response, with hazmat assistance potentially coming from Kansas City, St. Louis, or Jefferson City — hours away. “If Springfield fire is out on two and a half house fires, their resources are tapped out as well,” said Nelson Prewitt, chief of the Ebenezer Fire Protection District. County staff had recommended approval with eight conditions; both commissioners present voted to deny, consistent with the unanimous recommendation of the Greene County Planning Board in February.

title: Proposed solar farm in Sumter County withdrawn amid community pushback
snippet: TOCE SC Solar One, a subsidiary of Texas-based Treaty Oak Clean Energy, has withdrawn its application with the South Carolina Public Service Commission to build and operate the White Palmetto Solar Project — a 170-megawatt, 1,700-acre solar facility proposed near the intersection of Borden and Black River roads, about 12 miles northwest of the city of Sumter. The withdrawal came on March 20, two days after a public hearing at which residents voiced concerns about the project’s impact on farmable land and what they described as the developer’s failure to act as a good neighbor. The project had already been denied a special exception by the Sumter County Zoning Board of Appeals in May 2025, with the company subsequently appealing to the state PSC over the county’s objection. “They kept trying to say that, you know, we want to be good neighbors. But all of their actions showed that they really didn’t,” said Tanya Eddins, who spoke at the March 18 hearing. State Sen. Jeff Zell said the episode is expected to prompt broader discussion about how solar projects are regulated across South Carolina.

title: Judge vacates permit for 810-acre Northern Waters Solar project in Cheboygan County
snippet: A judge has vacated the special use permit granted by the Cheboygan County Planning Commission for the Northern Waters Solar Park, a 110-megawatt, 810-acre solar facility proposed by EDP Renewables North America in Grant Township. The planning commission approved the permit in August 2025, but a group of 19 residents subsequently sued the county and EDP Renewables, arguing that the commission failed to make required findings of fact on each section of the zoning ordinance before granting approval. Judge Gauthier sided with plaintiffs, ruling that because the application was for a special use permit, the commission was required to address each applicable section — including sections 18 and 20 — even under a compatible renewable energy ordinance. The county had previously enacted a temporary moratorium on large-scale solar projects following an earlier court ruling, before the Board of Commissioners rescinded that ordinance. EDP Renewables had anticipated beginning construction in 2027 with power delivery to approximately 30,000 Michigan homes by 2028.

title: Tippecanoe County continues debate on new solar farm ordinance
snippet: With a one-year moratorium on large-scale solar projects set to expire in June, the Tippecanoe County Area Plan Commission held its second of three public meetings on a proposed new 12-page solar ordinance. The most contested provisions include a 400-acre cap on individual projects, a 6,000-acre countywide maximum, setback distances from non-participating property lines, and a proposed outright ban on battery energy storage systems co-located with solar developments. The moratorium was triggered in part by the proposed 120-megawatt, 1,700-acre Rainbow Trout Solar Project in western Tippecanoe County, which was rejected by the Board of Zoning Appeals in August 2025 on a 4-3 vote and is now in litigation. The full Area Plan Commission is scheduled to hold a public hearing on the proposed ordinance on April 15, with a vote by county commissioners expected in May or June.

title: Why Doesn’t Texas, the Leader of Onshore Wind Energy, Have Any Offshore?
snippet: Inside Climate News reports that Texas state officials have led a sustained and largely successful effort to block offshore wind development in the Gulf of Mexico, even as the state generates more than a fifth of the country’s onshore wind power. The key barrier is political: the Texas General Land Office, whose approval is required for any offshore cable landing or substation, signaled to investors years ago that it would not grant such permits. When the Biden administration auctioned two Gulf lease sites off the Texas coast in 2023, neither attracted bids. Meanwhile, five offshore wind projects off the East Coast resumed construction this month after a federal judge blocked the Trump administration’s stop-work order — but Texas has none in the mix. Studies show the region’s offshore wind potential is comparable to its onshore success, and the state’s existing oil and gas workforce could transfer to the sector. “It signaled to investors that Texas was a risky place to invest,” said Stacy Ortego of the National Wildlife Federation.

title: Scoop
snippet: This GOP Lawmaker Is Aiming to Stop an Arizona Wind Farm: Republican Rep. Eli Crane is lobbying three separate federal agencies — the FAA, the Fish and Wildlife Service, and the FCC — to block Repsol’s Lava Run wind project, a proposed 500-megawatt wind farm in the White Mountains of Apache County, Arizona, according to audio from a late-March tele-town hall posted to social media. Crane told attendees he opposes the project and is doing everything he can to get Trump administration officials to intercede. The project is sited entirely on state and county land, which Crane acknowledged makes federal intervention challenging, but he noted Lava Run is likely to require federal permits for disturbing protected bird species — creating a potential avenue for the administration to block future construction. Apache County is also in the process of updating its renewable energy ordinance. Repsol is separately developing a 450-megawatt solar project on the same site.

title: California just hit an inflection point for batteries
snippet: On March 29 at 7pm, California’s grid batteries discharged 12.3 gigawatts of power — roughly equivalent to six Hoover Dams, and approximately 43% of total grid demand at that moment — setting a new state record. Nearly all of that storage capacity was built in the last five years, driven by sharp cost declines: battery costs have fallen 99% over three decades, with roughly a third of that drop coming in just the past few years. The milestone reflects how battery storage has unlocked a structural shift in California’s grid, allowing midday solar surplus to be captured and discharged during evening peak hours, reducing reliance on gas peaker plants and imported power. California’s grid now carries 17 gigawatts of battery storage and is targeting more than 50 gigawatts. “There’s a huge, huge additional amount that’s still going to come online over the next few years,” said Nicolas Fulghum, senior energy and climate data analyst at Ember.

title: Solar farm near Berlin moving forward – OC Today-Dispatch

title: Offshore wind energy more reliable, affordable for New England ratepayers, report finds
snippet: “[A] report, released by the Union of Concerned Scientists in February, found that offshore wind energy could have significantly lowered the risk of a blackout in the New England winter season. Expensive resources, like natural gas peaker plants, are typically deployed when the energy grid is stressed. Prices can spike rapidly and ratepayers “bear the burden.” Stephanie Francoeur, senior vice president of communications at the Oceantic Network, said Vineyard Wind and Revolution Wind could have saved over $800 per megawatt-hour during January’s winter storm.”

title: Bill would require data centers in Illinois to invest in clean energy or pay more
snippet: “A sweeping new proposal in Springfield would force data centers to disclose more about their water use, energy consumption, and environmental impact while tying them more directly into Illinois’ clean‑energy transition. Senate Bill 3830 creates a data center self‑direct program allowing operators to cut their renewable‑energy charges if they help bring new clean‑energy projects online. To qualify, data centers must sign long-term contracts with new wind, solar, or storage facilities and retire the environmental credits tied to them. Some facilities could offset up to 90% of mandated renewable‑energy costs”

title: Michigan approves 1,332MW of BESS with 332MW supporting Oracle data centre
snippet: “The Michigan Public Service Commission has approved six battery energy storage system (BESS) projects totalling 1,332MW of capacity in the US state. The data centre-related BESS projects approved by the MPSC include the initial 332MW out of 1,383MW of company-owned energy storage facilities that Green Chile Ventures (Oracle) must develop to meet the data centre’s contractual demand. The total capacity of the battery storage projects approved for this data centre exceeds the capacity of DTE Electric’s 1,150MW Blue Water Energy Centre, Michigan’s latest natural gas-fired plant, which was approved by the MPSC in 2018.”

title: Arizona cuts key renewables policy as clean energy market takes off
snippet: “Sunny Arizona closed out 2025 as the second-biggest state for battery and solar construction. Now, a policy that helped kick-start this success could be going away. The Arizona Corporation Commission, the elected body that regulates utilities, unanimously voted in early March to eliminate the state’s renewable portfolio standard. The policy, which the commission set in 2006, called for 15% renewable electricity by 2025. The state hit that target; thus, in the words of Commissioner Kevin Thompson, it was time to move beyond ​“mandates that have outlived their useful life.””

title: How tiny Kit Carson County has grown into a green energy giant
snippet: “ Kit Carson County is home to eight wind farms generating about 2.9 terawatt-hours of electricity a year- That figure is set to rise with the two projects under construction and at least four more wind farms in various stages of permitting and planning. The county is also in the process of permitting a solar installation. title: Utah regulators OK program meant to promote increased use of renewable energy
snippet: “ Utah regulators have given the green light to a new program in the works for seven years that's meant to promote increased use of renewable energy sources to power the state. The Utah Renewable Communities initiative, rooted in legislation passed by Utah lawmakers in 2019, HB411 , calls for the participation of as many as 19 Utah communities in a partnership with Rocky Mountain Power to promote renewable energy. Now it will be up to elected leaders in the 19 communities to adopt ordinances by June 2 formalizing their intent to take part.”

title: Despite roadblocks, Indiana's solar capacity nearly doubled last year
snippet: “All in all, the state's solar capacity nearly doubled in 2025, according to a report from Wood Mackenzie and the Solar Energy Industries Association, a trade group. Indiana saw the 3rd largest solar capacity influx in the country last year, behind energy giants Texas and California…And finding physical space to put the panels remains an ordeal throughout the country: one rough night in a county zoning meeting can halt a project years-in-the-making…[Topher Anderson, the director of programs and operations for the Indiana Conservative Alliance for Energy] worries that projects will continue to get caught in the crossfire of local anti-renewable sentiment, but ultimately, he thinks that solar opportunities will prove too alluring for Indiana to ignore.”

title: Missouri Solar Fight Puts Farmers’ Futures, and Land, at Stake
snippet: “Legislation under discussion would either temporarily halt new solar projects or impose additional requirements on future developments, including local approval, acreage limits, and changes to tax policy. Supporters say the proposals would allow the state to establish clearer rules. Opponents warn they could disrupt projects already underway and create uncertainty for rural landowners…For Callaway County farmer Fred Atkinson, the debate reflects a reality he has been navigating for decades…Through conservation practices, he reduced soil loss and rebuilt productivity on land that had been worn down over generations…Despite those improvements, Atkinson said the economics of farming have become increasingly difficult to sustain…A statewide moratorium could halt projects already in development, while additional requirements such as acreage limits or increased taxes could make future projects financially unworkable, they said. Others warned that shifting policy could discourage long-term investment in Missouri at a time when neighboring states are competing for energy projects.”

title: Observers say a plan to convert 136,000 acres of farmland into a 21,000 MW solar-plus-storage complex in California’s Central Valley can be a model for clean energy development in the state and nation
snippet: “The Valley Clean Infrastructure Plan (VCIP) envisions converting 136,000 acres of land into 21 gigawatts of battery-backed solar power — nearly as much utility-scale solar capacity as has been installed in California to date…None of this has happened yet, and completing it will take 10 years or more. But after years of work with developer Golden State Clean Energy, VCIP is now poised to move from concept to reality. In December, Westlands Water District’s board approved the programmatic environmental impact report that lays out a master plan for the project…Water shortages are the primary reason that Westlands growers are seeking alternatives to farming.”

title: East County green energy boom sparks concern over impacts to natural landscape, cultural sites
snippet: “East County has become the region’s epicenter in the renewable energy development boom…John Bathke, an assistant professor of Native American and Indigenous Studies at San Diego City College, characterized the trend in East County as ‘green colonialism.’ The term refers to the imposition of renewable energy projects on disadvantaged and Indigenous communities…Starlight Solar is a proposed 588-acre solar and battery storage project in Boulevard that would transform sections of natural space nestled between the Old Highway 80 and the U.S.-Mexico border…Durckel said ‘it’s possible’ the project may go to the county’s Planning Commission on May 8.”

title: California’s SB 886 could make storage a prerequisite for data center interconnection
snippet: “A new bill making its way through the California State Senate could effectively make energy storage a prerequisite for data centers to interconnect. The California Technology Innovation and Ratepayer Protection Act (SB 886) aims to protect ratepayers from cost impacts of data centers by ensuring that large load users pay the entirety of the grid costs they trigger. In practice, it could turn storage into a de-facto box on the interconnection checklist despite its framing as a ratepayer protection bill. While a previous version of the bill laid out a strict onsite behind-the-meter storage mandate, the latest text instead includes a requirement that developers of large projects – defined in the bill as those being 25 MW or larger – cover half of their hourly energy needs with dispatchable zero-carbon supply. This must be prefunded and pre-committed for a 15-year time period.”

title: Supervisors hit pause on energy storage projects
snippet: “...[T]he San Benito County Board of Supervisors has temporarily frozen approvals for large-scale energy storage projects. At its March 10 meeting, the board voted 4-1 to impose a 45-day moratorium, which can be extended to nearly two years, to allow the county to draft rules alongside stakeholders and the one company currently pursuing a local project, Allium Solar. title: Proposed Henderson County wind farm ignites fight over rural identity Proposed Henderson County wind farm ignites fight over rural identity
snippet: “A proposed wind energy project that could become Kentucky’s first utility-scale wind farm has sparked intense debate in Henderson County…At a March 18 public hearing, the Henderson City-County Planning Commission began a yearlong series of meetings to craft new land-use and siting rules for wind turbines while a county moratorium on new wind projects remains in place. The hearing focused on where and under what conditions such development could be allowed…The commission is scheduled to hold additional hearings on noise, shadow flicker and other issues, with the next one set for May 20. Once rules are drafted, the Planning Commission will make a recommendation to the Henderson Fiscal Court.For now, the moratorium prevents any new wind applications while the county decides how – or whether – to allow them.”

title: Jerome County, Idaho’s commissioners propose restricting utility-scale solar and wind projects to industrial zones
snippet: “Large-scale renewable energy projects on public lands, like Lava Ridge, are largely on hold… Jerome County is crafting a new energy ordinance with hopes of striking a balance between public wishes and private property rights. Under the existing ordinance, energy projects are allowed in any zoning type in Jerome County, including agricultural. However, commissioners have heard steady public opposition to industrial energy proposals for wind and solar, which have multiplied in recent years…In May 2025, the commission put a moratorium on energy projects while they developed a new ordinance governing private lands. Under the draft ordinance, energy production would only be allowed in industrial zones…An open house for the comprehensive plan and energy ordinance is on Monday, March 23, and a hearing on the energy ordinance is scheduled for April 13.”

title: Residents press McKean County commissioners on solar projects, ordinance still pending
snippet: “Residents asked commissioners to consider banning the project and issuing a moratorium on solar development in the county. According to Pennsylvania’s Municipalities Planning Code, county commissioners do not have the authority to take either action; zoning and land‑use decisions, including any moratorium, must be enacted by the township that holds zoning power. Back in October, however, when commissioners were working to adopt the county’s wind energy ordinance, they indicated the county was working on similar protective ordinances for projects such as solar and battery storage. Five months later, commissioners have said the county is in the final stages of completing its ordinance for adoption.”

title: Dame discusses possibility of wind mills moving into the county
snippet: “During Thursday's [McLean County] Fiscal Court meeting, Dame and the four county magistrates voted to allow County Attorney Donna Dant to begin drafting a moratorium concerning the potential windmill construction in the county. The draft, once approved, will be sent to the members of the McLean County Planning and Zoning Committee for any changes, before being presented to the public via a public hearing. The moratorium will amend the existing ordinance to prohibit the construction of windmills in the county for a period of one year, while additional information is gathered about the impact of the windmills on the county.”

title: Amazon buys site of Oregon’s largest planned solar and energy storage project
snippet: “After outbidding regional utilities with an $83 million offer in January, Amazon acquired the site of a planned 1,200-megawatt solar project and up to 7,200-megawatt battery storage capacity development late last month... But the $2.3 trillion tech company still has state regulatory hurdles to clear before it can begin construction, and a tight timeline if the company plans to take advantage of expiring federal solar tax credits. The Sunstone Solar Project… is slated to be the largest solar and battery storage project so far in Oregon, with plans to be located on private land in Morrow County… Oregon Department of Energy’s senior policy advisor for siting, Sarah Esterson, said the agency has been in contact with Pine Gate Renewables about transferring the site certificate since November…. It could take between three and six months, according to Esterson… The Oregon Energy Facility Siting Council has reviewed and approved dozens of requests to transfer in the past, she said, but an entity like Amazon would be a first for the state.”

title: Vacaville Moves to Block Lithium-Ion Battery Storage Projects
snippet: “The Vacaville City Council voted unanimously on Tuesday to pass an ordinance that aims to severely limit Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) in the city, culminating a year-long effort on the issue. Since the last council discussion of the ordinance, city staff updated it to disallow Lithium-ion battery chemistry in the city. Multiple BESS projects in the area are already working their way through the California Energy Commission’s AB 205 alternate permitting pathway, which would supersede the city’s ordinance if approved.”

title: Are faked public comments about to tank an Ohio solar farm?
snippet: Open Road Renewables’ 94-megawatt Crossroads Solar Grazing Center in Morrow County — a dual-use agrivoltaic project pairing solar generation with sheep grazing about an hour north of Columbus — is facing potential denial by the Ohio Power Siting Board despite an investigation by Canary Media finding that a substantial number of public comments opposing the project appear to have been fabricated. Company vice president Doug Herling reviewed comments submitted to the siting board and identified at least 34 instances of apparently false names or addresses; Canary Media independently verified the existence of only one of those commenters. After removing fake, anonymous, and duplicate submissions, Herling found that more than 78% of verifiable public comments supported the project, with support running above 58% within Morrow County itself. The anti-solar group Concerned Citizens of Morrow County had publicly claimed opposition ran 10-to-1 — a figure that did not hold up under cross-examination during the seven-day evidentiary hearing. The siting board’s staff reversed its earlier recommendation in favor of the project after a last-minute resolution by Cardington Township, where a single trustee changed his vote. The board is expected to rule by March 19. “When comments are submitted under false identities, false addresses, or with misrepresented affiliations, it undermines the credibility of the process and is unfair to residents who participated honestly and in good faith,” said Bella Bogin, director of programs at Ohio Citizen Action.

title: Bill to pause new solar developments advances to Alabama Senate for vote
snippet: The Alabama Senate Transportation and Energy Committee voted to advance Senate Bill 354, sponsored by Sen. Greg Albritton, R-Atmore, which would impose a one-year moratorium on the construction of new large-scale solar power facilities statewide — with an amendment carving out territory served by the Tennessee Valley Authority. The bill was prompted by community backlash over a proposed $300 million, 4,500-acre Silicon Ranch solar project in Stockton, in north Baldwin County, intended to help power a Meta data center. “The public is very concerned with what the reclamation requirements are, if any. They’re concerned about any ecological matters that come into play, and lots of questions that I don’t know how to answer,” Albritton said. “I’m asking that we pass this moratorium on construction of any new ones, until we can find and get some answers as to those policies and procedures.” The TVA carve-out was introduced by Sen. Steve Livingston, R-Scottsboro, citing concerns about Redstone Arsenal: “The last thing you want is a war fighter to have a little hiccup, because we’ve had a little power interruption.” The sole public hearing speaker, John Dodd of Energy Alabama, opposed the bill, arguing it amounts to hitting “a kill switch on an entire industry overnight” rather than fixing the process failures that enabled the Stockton project. “If the process failed the public, then we should fix the process,” Dodd said. The committee advanced the bill with Sens. Robert Stewart, D-Selma, and Merika Coleman, D-Birmingham, in opposition. If enacted, the moratorium would take effect immediately and would not apply to solar farms already built or under construction.

title: Solar farm proposal earns ‘unfavorable’ recommendation
snippet: The Montgomery County Advisory Plan Commission voted 6-2 to send an unfavorable recommendation to the Montgomery County Board of Commissioners on a rezoning request tied to the proposed Copper Box Solar Project. The petition, filed Jan. 13 by ENGIE North America, seeks to rezone about 1,800 acres of agricultural land across 19 parcels in Madison and Union townships to an agricultural overlay district to allow construction of a commercial solar energy system, with approximately 890 acres within fenced solar array areas. Lead project developer Tom Schoder said the project would generate up to 200 MW of electricity — enough to power about 33,000 homes — represent a roughly $300 million investment, generate at least $20 million in tax revenue over its 30-year lifespan, create about 250 temporary construction jobs, and connect to a Duke Energy transmission line feeding into a Crawfordsville substation. Opponents raised concerns about removing productive farmland from agricultural use, potential fire hazards, environmental impacts, and possible effects on nearby property values. The commission voted to send an unfavorable recommendation based on three factors: the request was inconsistent with the comprehensive plan, inconsistent with the current conditions and character of the area, and inconsistent with the most desirable use for which the land is adapted. The Montgomery County Board of Commissioners will make the final decision at a future meeting following a public hearing.

title: Harrington adopts ordinance prohibiting utility‑scale solar farms
snippet: Voters at Harrington’s annual town meeting enacted a Solar Energy Ordinance banning utility-scale solar and for-profit power generation, with the measure drawing overwhelming support and no objections from the roughly 35-person audience gathered at Narraguagus High School. The ordinance preserves personal and commercial on-site solar with Planning Board approval, and permits net metering for energy offset — but not for profit. Its stated intent is to preserve rural character and “prevent industrial encroachment incompatible with community values.” Ground-mounted systems are subject to strict dimensional limits: maximum 25 feet tall, 25-foot setbacks from property lines, 45 feet from town roads, and 70 feet from state roads, with a maximum 2,500 square feet of airspace coverage.

title: Toxic waste cleanup may delay Washington County solar farm
snippet: St. Louis-based Cornerstone Solar LLC has applied to build a 200 MW solar farm on an abandoned strip mine in Jefferson Township, Washington County — the same site where the EPA is now moving to remove approximately 4,500 barrels of toxic waste illegally dumped and buried since the late 1970s. EPA on-scene coordinator Kevin Clark updated residents at a March 6 community meeting at the Eldersville Fire Hall, saying the cleanup — capped at $2 million in Superfund money — will proceed independently of the solar permitting process, and that the EPA is not involved in overseeing the solar farm application. The township is holding a planning commission meeting and public hearing on the Cornerstone application.

title: 50,000-acre west Michigan wind turbine plan dead after years of debate
snippet: Apex Clean Energy has abandoned its Montcalm Wind Project after failing to secure enough land leases, falling roughly 20,000 acres short of its 50,000-acre goal across up to 11 townships in Montcalm County. The project generated years of heated local opposition, including board recalls, sheriff’s deputies at public meetings, and repeated clashes before planning commissions. Farmers who had signed leases saw the project as a significant income opportunity, while opponents raised concerns over property values, setbacks, noise, and turbine height. Apex secured commitments from around 500 landowners before pulling out.

title: Crete Village Board annexes property for construction of 2,400-acre solar farm
snippet: The Crete Village Board voted 5-1 on March 10 to annex approximately 200 acres and approve construction of a 2,400-acre, 260 MW solar farm by Earthrise Energy (Arlington, VA) in the Plum Valley area, spanning both the village and unincorporated Will County. The company already operates an existing natural gas plant in Crete and plans to use that infrastructure for grid connection. The project is expected to power roughly 50,000 Illinois households, with construction anticipated later in 2026 pending county approvals and grid connection targeted for 2028. The vote drew strong resident backlash; attorney Steven Becker said a client may have grounds to sue over notice violations, while board member Holly Milburn defended the outreach effort, saying the board had sent mailers and visited 172 households.

title: Wisconsin bill would give municipalities say on wind, solar projects
snippet: A public hearing was held March 10 before the Senate Committee on Utilities, Technology and Tourism on Senate Bill 3, which would require wind and solar projects of 15 MW or more to obtain approval from every affected local municipality before the Public Service Commission can act. Under current law, projects under 100 MW require local approval, while those at 100 MW or above go directly to the PSC. Municipalities would have 90 days to approve or deny a project under the bill; failure to act would constitute automatic approval. Supporters included local officials, the Wisconsin Farm Bureau, Realtors, and the MacIver Institute. Opponents included utilities, environmental groups, trade unions, and the Conservative Energy Forum, which argued the bill fragments energy planning and threatens grid reliability. Co-sponsor Sen. Howard Marklein (R-Spring Green) said the bill targets solar and wind specifically because “those were the two projects that we heard about” from constituents.

title: Avangrid installs all panels at 120 MW Oregon solar park
snippet: Avangrid has completed installation of more than 250,000 solar panels — assembled by SEG Solar at its Houston facility — at the Tower Solar project in Morrow County, located just west of Boardman on approximately 900 acres of industrially zoned land owned by the Port of Morrow. The project totals 166 MWdc / 120 MWac and is expected to reach commercial operation later in 2026, delivering energy to the Portland General Electric grid via the Green Future Impact voluntary program and supporting a new QTS data center campus. The project created approximately 200 construction jobs, mostly regional union labor, and is expected to generate roughly $20 million in combined PILOTs and property taxes. title: Creekstone secures land for 1 GW of solar to power Utah data centre
snippet: Creekstone Energy has executed a long-term solar lease with Utah’s School and Institutional Trust Lands Administration for approximately 13,000 acres in Millard County, with Millard County granting zoning approval for the first phase. The company plans to develop more than 1 GW of solar over the next 18 months to power its Delta Gigasite — a master-planned AI hyperscale data center campus of more than 1,100 acres designed for 10+ GW of total capacity. Lease revenue will benefit Utah’s public education system. CEO Ray Conley said: “We are building more than a data center campus — we are building an energy ecosystem.” COO Chase Sheffield added: “With 13,000 acres now under lease, we have the land position to build a solar portfolio that materially offsets the power demands of our hyperscale tenants.” First solar generation is expected within one year, with data center deliveries beginning in 2027.

title: Vacaville adopts Battery Energy Storage Systems ordinance
snippet: The Vacaville City Council voted unanimously on March 10 to adopt a BESS ordinance, the culmination of a year-long community process. Key provisions include a citywide ban on lithium-ion battery chemistry, increased setbacks from 300 to 500 feet for sensitive uses, and a required financial assurance plan; one parcel — the former Gibson Canyon Creek Wastewater Treatment Plant — is retained for potential non-lithium BESS development. Multiple BESS projects are already working through the California Energy Commission’s AB 205 alternate permitting pathway, which could supersede the city ordinance. Derek Johnson, speaking on behalf of Keep Vacaville Safe’s Sarah Dunn, said the ordinance “represents years of careful work by the city staff, residents and this council to understand the benefits, risks and long-term impacts of large-scale lithium-ion BESS facilities in Vacaville.”

title: Gloversville considers ban on battery storage facilities
snippet: The Gloversville Common Council discussed, on March 10, a proposed ban on commercial battery energy storage systems, and may set a public hearing for March 24. Mayor Gary Antonucci said: "It hasn't been proven enough that it's safe enough. It's the safety of our residents." The draft law states the city finds that "utility-scale solar energy systems and commercial battery energy storage systems may have significant impacts on land use patterns, public safety, infrastructure capacity, emergency response preparedness, environmental conditions, and the long-term redevelopment objectives of the city." Exceptions would be made for battery systems serving on-site residential or commercial buildings without re-selling power, and for solar installations generating no more than 110% of on-site consumption. At least 13 municipalities in the Greater Capitol Region have enacted or plan to enact a moratorium or ban. Nearby, a 10 MW BESS in Northampton has already been approved; the same developer, Carson Power, proposes a 20 MW system in St. Armand in Essex County.

title: Town keeps battery storage moratorium
snippet: The Town of Dunkirk in Chautauqua County voted unanimously to extend its moratorium on battery energy storage systems by an additional six months to allow more time to draft a local law. Town Board member Phil Leone, who initially proposed the local law, said: "We're on our way to creating our new battery energy storage system law, but we just need some additional time." There is currently no BESS proposal before the board. Supervisor Shari Miller said: "We are not doing this in a vacuum. We have a lot of people that have their hands on it." The board has another workshop scheduled for March 26 at 5:30 p.m. Nearby, the Town of Hanover has already passed a local law banning BESS entirely following a proposed project that drew significant public outcry.

title: Proposed makeover of utility regulator met with warnings consumers would lose their voice
snippet: A Republican priority bill, Senate Bill 8, would expand the Kentucky Public Service Commission from three to five members — with two new appointments going to the Republican state auditor rather than the Democratic governor — and would administratively attach the PSC to the auditor's office. Critics warned the bill would make it harder for independent consumer advocates to intervene in utility rate cases, including those involving renewable energy. Former PSC Chair Kent Chandler said the measure "raises the bar for intervention, limits the likelihood of intervention, and ultimately reduces the evidence available to the commission in making their decisions." The Kentucky Resources Council called it a "calculated effort to silence independent advocacy for residential customers." After pushback, sponsor Sen. Brandon Smith (R-Hazard) stripped the intervention barriers before the GOP-controlled Senate passed the modified bill 30-5 on March 7. One clean energy advocate noted the bill still does not address existing laws that make it "nearly impossible" for utilities to propose lower-cost renewable alternatives to aging fossil fuel plants.

title: Michigan township seeking control over project that could add 1,000 acres of solar panels
snippet: Grass Lake Charter Township in Jackson County is pushing to retain local authority over NextEra Energy's proposed 100 MW Grass Lake Solar project, which would span approximately 700–1,000 acres across Grass Lake and Leoni Townships. Under Michigan's Public Act 233, enacted in 2023, the state's Public Service Commission can approve large-scale solar projects of 50 MW or more, potentially bypassing local ordinances it deems incompatible or "unworkable." Township Supervisor John Lesinski warned: "They might honestly come to a point where we can't agree on a final draft, at which case they could go to the MPSC and bypass a local community." Planning Commission Chair Tim Golding added: "Because if they don't want to work with us, they're just going to go to the state anyway, and they're going to put it wherever they want it." The project would generate approximately $15 million in local tax revenues and 100–200 construction jobs.

title: Battery storage moratorium extended
snippet: Klickitat County Commissioners extended a moratorium on battery energy storage systems — and solar — for another six months at their March 3 meeting. Planning Director Scott Edelman reviewed the history: the moratorium was originally passed in September 2024 to allow time to develop standards and an ordinance, and has been extended multiple times since. The planning commission was comfortable with a draft in December, but asked for a legal review by the prosecutor's office; former PA David Quesnel then resigned, and his replacement has not yet had time to provide feedback. Edelman said: "I don't anticipate another extension."

title: Arizona Corporation Commission ends state's renewable energy standard
snippet: The Arizona Corporation Commission voted unanimously on March 4 to repeal the state's Renewable Energy Standard and Tariff rules, which since 2006 had required utilities to source 15% of their power from renewables by 2025. Arizona Public Service, Tucson Electric Power, and UniSource Energy Services have collectively collected more than $2.3 billion in REST surcharges from customers. The ACC said the mandates "are no longer needed and the costs are no longer justified," arguing utilities are also saddled with above-market solar contracts entered into to comply with the rules. Commission Chair Nick Myers said repealing REST does not mean renewable energy will disappear: "Any generation source, whether it be wind, solar, natural gas, whatever, is going to have to bid for their place in the market on an even playing field." Critics argued the repeal ignores the documented benefits of the program, including reduced pollution and more affordable long-term energy. Prior to the vote, APS had already dropped its voluntary goal of reaching 100% clean energy by 2050, announcing instead a major investment in natural gas.

title: Planning commission recommends extension of BESS moratorium
snippet: The Henderson City-County Planning Commission voted unanimously to recommend extending a proposed one-year moratorium on battery energy storage systems to two years, with the clock starting only after the Henderson County Fiscal Court formally approves it — potentially pushing the start to April or May. The fiscal court originally passed a resolution for a one-year BESS moratorium in January, after voting to begin the process at a December meeting. Planning Commission Chair David Dixon said the purpose of the moratorium is to have more time to "amend or rewrite the current (BESS) ordinance." Airline Road resident Deirdre McConathy, whose family farm abuts a plot of land where a BESS had been proposed before the company dropped its plans in December, has been a vocal supporter of the moratorium and advocated for the longer two-year timeframe. The fiscal court will now decide whether to accept the two-year recommendation, maintain its original one-year proposal, or chart a different course.

title: Concerned Citizens of the Ozarks speaks out against wind turbines
snippet: Residents opposing a proposed 200 MW wind project in Washington County gathered at the Lincoln Community Center on February 23 as Concerned Citizens of the Ozarks pushed back against renewable energy company RES's early-stage Trillium Wind Project, north of Lincoln. George Caudle, a retired Carroll County attorney, urged justices of the peace from the Washington County Quorum Court who attended the meeting to "seriously consider what protections in the county need to be in place in the event of a wind project." JP Willie Leming of Lincoln said Washington County is already a step ahead of most counties because it uses a conditional use permit process, but acknowledged "it still has work to do." The 34-turbine, 200 MW project would power roughly 40,000 homes according to the company; RES said it is committed to engaging with the local community and that "local knowledge and feedback are vital in shaping any new development."

title: Public to weigh in as Morrison County considers pausing some renewable energy projects
snippet: The Morrison County Board of Commissioners has scheduled a public hearing for April 7 to vote on whether to adopt a one-year moratorium on new solar gardens, solar farms, and wind energy conversion systems of certain sizes — driven by growing interest in large-scale renewable projects and the need to update county ordinances before more projects advance. Land Services Director Amy Kowalzek stressed the moratorium would not affect small-scale residential or farm systems, nor projects large enough to fall under the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission's jurisdiction. "Those are the three things that we're looking at putting a moratorium on — not the small-scale ground mount, like if someone's going to put three panels up for their personal use, that's not what we're talking about here," Kowalzek said. The county also proposed a working group of commissioners, planning commission members, an industry representative and a county resident to develop updated ordinance language.

title: Shasta Power seeks lifting of solar moratorium in Crawford County
snippet: Shasta Power presented survey results to the Crawford County Commission showing strong local support for its proposed solar project, but commissioners indicated the current moratorium on solar development will remain in place until a comprehensive county plan is completed. The company surveyed 37 residents living immediately adjacent to the proposed solar site north of 620th Avenue and reported that more than 70% supported the project. "It tells you that people want this to happen. They just want clarity. So we're asking the county for clarity," said co-founder and managing partner Max Roe. Commissioners told Shasta Power that no decision can be made until the county completes its comprehensive planning process; the moratorium is set to expire in December but could end earlier. Owner-manager John Copyak expressed concern about the timeline's uncertainty, saying delays could jeopardize tax credit eligibility: "My concern is this could take quite a long time. And we'd like to keep the project alive, making deposits and securing the tax credit." If approved, Copyak said the project represents a "$400 million investment in Crawford County." No MW capacity was disclosed.

title: Douglas County commissioners to consider one-year extension for North Lawrence solar farm permit
snippet: Douglas County commissioners are weighing a one-year extension for the Kansas Sky Energy Center near North Lawrence — a 1,105-acre project covering 8 million square feet that would power roughly 30,000 homes — after ongoing litigation has prevented construction from beginning. Grant Township and more than 20 businesses and residents sued in 2024 to overturn the commission's approval, alleging violations of zoning regulations, ignored flood risks and rushed permitting. A trial is not expected to begin until at least November 30, 2026. A representative for the project warned that without the extension, "denial would cause undue harm to the Applicant by effectively terminating the project." Commissioners also considered a funding agreement allowing the county to recover costs for third-party inspections tied to the conditional use permit.

title: Avantus Closes Over $300 Million in Construction Financing with BBVA and CIBC for Kitt Solar and Energy Storage Project
snippet: Avantus has closed a financing package of more than $300 million with BBVA and CIBC for the Kitt Solar and Energy Storage Project in Pinal County, currently under construction in the City of Eloy. The project features 100 MWac/130 MWdc of solar paired with 400 MWh of battery storage, with a PPA signed with Arizona Public Service and commercial operation expected by end of 2026. The financing includes construction funding, a tax equity bridge loan, and letters of credit, with RES serving as EPC and Fluence providing the battery system. "The financing demonstrates the continued bankability and commercial viability of utility-scale solar and storage projects in the region, and Avantus' growing presence in Arizona," said CEO Cliff Graham. The project is expected to create up to 500 construction jobs and generate over $25 million in local tax revenues once operational.

title: CT Siting Council Approves Expansion of Heavily Opposed Gravel Pit Solar
snippet: The Connecticut Siting Council voted unanimously 6-0 on March 5 to approve a 30 MW expansion of East Windsor's Gravel Pit Solar — the largest solar installation in Connecticut — adding 150 acres to the existing 120 MW project owned by New York-based DESRI Holdings. Opposition was fierce, with 2,223 petition signatures and pushback from state Sen. Saud Anwar. "East Windsor cannot absorb all of the solar in Connecticut," said Christina Dahl of opposition group East Windsor Citizens for Responsible Solar Development. DESRI Executive Director Aaron Svedlow said the project provides "lasting benefits for both the community and the state." Also in the pipeline: DESRI's proposed 100 MW Saltbox Solar project in East Windsor and Ellington.

title: Clayco Launches Solar & Storage Business Unit, Eyes Illinois Project
snippet: Chicago-based Clayco announced the launch of Clayco Power and Energy, a new business unit focused on utility-scale solar and battery storage, led by Ryan Johnson. The unit is expected to generate about $300 million in annual revenue and add nearly 1,000 employees by 2027. Clayco is currently designing a solar and battery storage project on 350 acres in Manteno — about an hour south of Chicago — with construction scheduled to start this June. CEO Anthony Johnson said "solar must be part of the solution" and "the fastest type of power to bring to the market." No MW capacity was disclosed for the Manteno project.

title: Google-Xcel Energy Deal Includes World's Largest Grid Battery
snippet: Google and Xcel Energy announced Feb. 24 plans to build what Xcel calls "the largest battery project by gigawatt-hour energy capacity announced to date in the world" — a 300 MW/30 GWh iron-air battery from Form Energy — to support a new Google data center in Pine Island, MN. The full package includes 1,400 MW of wind and 200 MW of solar, funded through a new Clean Energy Accelerator Charge tariff paid entirely by Google. Form Energy CEO Mateo Jaramillo said modules will ship by end of 2028, calling it "the scale of project that we expect to bid and participate in going forward." The tariff will be filed with Minnesota regulators in the coming weeks.

title: Zelestra Breaks Ground on Two Texas Solar Projects Totaling 441 MW
snippet: Zelestra commenced construction in January 2026 on the 253 MWdc Echols Grove facility in Lamar County and the 188 MWdc Cedar Range facility in Hopkins County, with McCarthy Building Companies as lead EPC contractor. The projects will support 400+ local jobs and ~$20 million in local economic activity.
